Paper Title: Rice yield and electricity production in agro-photovoltaic systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: Rice yield decreased (~20%), altered panicle/spikelet numbers, changes in head/broken rice rates, shifted physicochemical/taste profiles.

Renewable energy sources like solar power offer a viable alternative. This study explores the feasibility of agro-photovoltaic (APV) systems, which integrate solar panels with agricultural land to generate electricity while cultivating crops. Specifically, the impact of APV systems on rice production and quality was investigated. Solar modules with a total capacity of 99.84 kW were installed on a 2580 m2 site, with two module configurations tested. Two rice (Oryza sativa L.) cultivars, Woonkwang and Saenuri, were transplanted and monitored for growth characteristics, chlorophyll content, and fluorescence, as well as yield and quality. Growth analysis of rice under APV systems showed minimal impact on plant height and tiller numbers, though chlorophyll content analysis indicated delayed leaf aging and extended maturation time. Rice yield decreased due to altered panicle and spikelet numbers. Quality analysis revealed changes in head rice rate and broken rice, stressing optimal harvest timing in APV systems. Shading conditions also affected physicochemical properties and taste profiles. Yields dropped by about 20% under APV systems, but the financial returns from electricity generation significantly outweighed the crop revenue loss. Despite the high initial installation costs and regulatory challenges, APV systems present a promising dual-use approach for enhancing farm income and promoting renewable energy.; Fossil fuels, particularly oil, face sustainability challenges due to depletion and their role in increasing atmospheric CO2 levels, contributing to climate change and impacting global agriculture. Drivers of Risk: Fossil fuel dependence, CO2 emissions, climate change impact on agriculture, rice yield reduction under APV, altered rice quality due to shading.
Methodology: Solar modules (99.84 kW total) were installed on a 2580 m2 site in two configurations, and two rice cultivars (Woonkwang and Saenuri) were transplanted and monitored for growth characteristics, chlorophyll content, fluorescence, yield, and quality.
Highlights: Agro-photovoltaic (APV) systems integrating solar panels with rice cultivation show minimal impact on plant height and tiller numbers but decrease yield (around 20%) due to altered panicle and spikelet numbers; APV systems delay leaf aging and affect rice quality, physicochemical properties, and taste profiles, but financial returns from electricity generation outweigh crop revenue loss, suggesting potential for enhancing farm income and promoting renewable energy.
More Notes: Agro-photovoltaic (APV) systems integrating solar panels with rice cultivation show promise by generating electricity and potentially increasing farm income, although rice yields decreased by 20% and quality was altered, requiring optimized harvest timing.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is addressed through the exploration of agro-photovoltaic (APV) systems, which integrate solar energy with agriculture to enhance farm income and promote renewable energy use. Despite challenges such as reduced rice yields and regulatory hurdles, APV systems are considered a viable strategy for adapting to sustainability demands by providing financial returns from electricity that outweigh crop revenue losses. Further research and investment are emphasized for optimizing and expanding APV adoption.

Paper Title: A PAST, PRESENT, AND FUTURE OUTLOOK ON THE MISSISSIPPI OYSTER FISHERY
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decline in oyster landings; recruitment limitation; hampered early life history; reduced reef resilience.

Drivers of Risk: Hurricane Katrina, Deepwater Horizon oil spill, freshwater flooding, salinity variability, climate change-related precipitation, recruitment limitation, water quality alteration, environmental instability.
Methodology: A review of the Mississippi oyster fishery was conducted to characterize historical conditions relative to a sustainable resource, how the oyster resource has changed over time due to environmental factors and nonbiological events, and present-day challenges hindering oyster recovery efforts.
Highlights: Mississippi's oyster fishery, once a major economic driver, has drastically declined due to events like hurricanes, oil spills, and freshwater flooding; recent climate change-related precipitation events have further impacted water quality and oyster recruitment, hindering restoration efforts and requiring substantial management investment for future sustainability.
More Notes: Climate change-related precipitation events have negatively impacted oyster recruitment and reef resilience in Mississippi, hindering recovery efforts despite significant restoration investments.
Adaptation considered: Cultch planting efforts have historically aided recovery. Significant resources have been invested in oyster restoration in the northern Gulf of Mexico. Successful restoration requires management practices providing suitable substrate and enhancing existing stock.

Drivers of Risk: Hydrological (flood extent/intensity), vulnerable WASH infrastructure/populations, socio-economic factors.
Methodology: Employs a multi-step framework integrating hydrological modeling (predicting flood extents/intensities using historical data/climate projections), GIS-based risk assessment (mapping vulnerable WASH infrastructure/populations), and socio-economic analysis (assessing impacts on community health/livelihoods), incorporating a decision-support system for prioritizing interventions.
Highlights: Floods significantly compromise water quality and access, disrupt sanitation services, and increase waterborne diseases, disproportionately affecting vulnerable communities; the study offers a framework for resilient WASH systems, enhanced community preparedness, and reduced flood-related health risks, adaptable to various contexts.
More Notes: Floods threaten WASH systems globally, contaminating water, damaging sanitation, and disrupting hygiene, leading to increased waterborne diseases; a new framework integrates hydrological modeling, risk assessment, and socio-economic analysis to mitigate these vulnerabilities and aid in designing resilient WASH systems.
Adaptation considered: The framework aims to design resilient WASH systems and enhance community preparedness to reduce flood-related health risks. A decision-support system prioritizes interventions based on risk and resource availability.

Methodology: The study used CMIP6 data and a river ice model to predict global river ice changes in response to climate change.
Highlights: Global warming is predicted to significantly decrease river ice extent and duration, especially in North America, Europe, Siberia, and the Tibetan Plateau, with eastern Europe experiencing the greatest average loss and the most substantial changes occurring from October to June.
More Notes: Climate change, particularly in North America, Europe, Siberia, and the Tibetan Plateau, will significantly reduce river ice extent and duration, especially from October to June, with eastern Europe experiencing the greatest average loss.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in the abstract. The study focuses on projecting river ice changes under different climate scenarios.

Drivers of Risk: Chill requirements, climate adaptability, methodological variability, chill availability, production risks.
Methodology: Chill requirements (CR) of almond and apricot cultivars were estimated using six approaches: bud weight increase (30% and 50%), different percentages of bud break (30%, 50% and 90%) after forcing conditions, and Partial Least Square (PLS) regression, to compare experimental and statistical methods for CR estimation.
Highlights: Choice of method significantly affects chill requirement (CR) estimates for temperate fruit trees, impacting adaptation assessments under climate change scenarios, thus standardizing methods is crucial for reliable adaptation predictions.
More Notes: Different methods for estimating chilling requirements (CR) in fruit trees yield significantly different results, impacting assessments of future crop adaptation under climate change.
Adaptation considered: The study assesses adaptation potential by comparing the probability of fulfilling cultivar-specific chill requirements (CR) under global warming scenarios, based on CR estimates obtained through different methodologies. It highlights that the variability in CR quantification methods significantly impacts assessing the probability of successful adaptation.

Paper Title: Can today's and tomorrow's world uniformly gain from carbon taxation?
Outcome(s) of Interest: GDP reduction (high-damage scenarios); regional catastrophic damages (India, Brazil, South Asian Pacific); welfare gains (carbon tax + redistribution); fossil fuel use reduction; global emission reduction; welfare gains for all, present and future.

Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic climate change, temperature increases, fossil fuel utilization, regional economic policies, carbon policy participation (especially China), transition speed to clean energy substitutability, temperature deviations from optimal levels, productivity rate differentials, population dynamics, shape of damage function, lack of carbon taxation/transfers.
Methodology: The study employs a large-scale, multi-region overlapping generations integrated assessment model (OLG IAM), incorporating region-specific emissions and damages, calibrated with UN demographic data and using pattern scaling to assess climate impacts. The model integrates dirty and clean energy use, assuming varying substitutability and energy costs. Carbon taxation impacts are evaluated under different scenarios with a focus on Pareto-efficient solutions involving diverse assumptions on coal supply, substitutability, and coalition policies. Numerical methods like Gauss–Seidel are used to solve iteratively for optimal tax paths that ensure uniform welfare gains across generations and regions, leveraging the DICE model and climate emulator calibrated to CMIP5.
Highlights: A global carbon tax policy, especially involving China, is crucial for effectively mitigating climate change, with redistribution policies needed to equitably share welfare gains and limit burdens to less than 10 percent across regions and generations. Carbon taxation can reduce damages and emissions significantly, notably lowering regional GDP losses and increasing welfare by up to 4.3 percent. The policy leads to a marked transition to clean energy, balanced by necessary compensations to avoid welfare losses for current generations.
More Notes: The paper includes figures and tables providing data on regional GDP, energy consumption, CO2 emissions relative to the US, UN-projected demographics, region-specific social cost of carbon (SCC), and projected fossil fuel use under business-as-usual (BAU) and optimal policy scenarios.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is modeled through region-specific productivity functions that vary with temperature, allowing some regions to benefit from warming up to a point (Krusell–Smith-type productivity functions). The model explores different damage scenarios.

Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic climate change, temperature increases, fossil fuel utilization, regional economic policies, carbon policy participation (China), transition speed to clean energy, temperature deviations from optimal (T*), productivity/population dynamics, damage function shape, lack of carbon taxation/transfers, regional emissions.
Methodology: A large-scale, annually calibrated, multi-region, overlapping generations (OLG) integrated assessment model (IAM) is used, incorporating region-specific emissions, damages determined via pattern scaling, and calibrated to UN demographic data. The DICE model's functional form is adopted for climate evolution, with a parameterization from Folini et al. (2024), and regional temperatures are estimated using pattern scaling. Regional total factor productivity (TFP) is modeled with a climate emulator integrated with the economic model, iterating on guessed paths for variables until convergence is achieved using a Gauss-Seidel technique. Energy consumption data from the US Energy Information Administration (EIA) is used to calibrate CES production functions. A recursive solution algorithm solves one-period problems, and an iterative procedure determines optimal carbon tax paths based on the Social Cost of Carbon (SCC), utilizing the Auerbach and Kotlikoff Lump-Sum Redistribution Authority (LSRA) to implement pre-specified Pareto-efficient outcomes.
Highlights: Carbon taxation with redistribution can mitigate climate change damages and improve welfare, but requires global participation and faces challenges related to intergenerational and interregional equity.
More Notes: The paper references figures and tables detailing net transfers to cohorts (Fig. 4), optimal carbon taxes (Fig. 5), regional temperature changes under different policies, substitutability of clean for dirty energy (Fig. 10), GDP, energy consumption, and CO2 emissions by region relative to the US (Table 8), UN-projected demographics (Fig. 12), region-specific social cost of carbon (Tables 9 & 10), and projected fossil fuel use under different scenarios (Figs. 14, 15, & 16).
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is modeled implicitly through temperature impacts on total factor productivity (TFP), where some regions initially benefit from warming. Explicit adaptation measures are not included, but the analysis explores scenarios with varying degrees of substitutability between clean and dirty energy, which could be considered a form of technological adaptation. The model also examines the impact of different carbon tax policies and transfer schemes, with the goal of achieving Pareto efficiency and uniform welfare gains.

Outcome(s) of Interest: Agrivoltaics, conservation agriculture, food security, energy production, climate resilience, soil health, biodiversity, carbon sequestration, economic benefits.

It could enhance the ecological benefits of AV by improving soil health and biodiversity. It could create economic opportunities for farmers and increase the resilience and diversity of food crops under changing climate conditions. Furthermore, it could inform stakeholders about the benefits and challenges of implementing conservation agriculture management practices (CAMP) in AV and encourage further exploration and adoption of this innovative approach. ... Methodology: Proposes an ecosystem approach integrating renewable energy (agrivoltaics) and sustainable food production using conservation agriculture management practices (CAMP), encouraging research on the effects of CAMP on agrivoltaic systems.
Highlights: Conservation-agrivoltaics (Conservation-AV) integrates agrivoltaics (AV) with conservation agriculture management practices (CAMP) to enhance sustainable food and energy production, improve soil health and biodiversity, create economic opportunities for farmers, increase climate resilience and crop diversity, promote carbon sequestration and lower emissions, and inform stakeholders about the benefits and challenges of CAMP in AV.
More Notes: The integration of agrivoltaics (AV) with conservation agriculture management practices (CAMP) aims to sustainably enhance food and energy production by improving soil health, biodiversity, and resilience to climate change, while offering economic opportunities for farmers and promoting long-term environmental benefits.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)

Adoption of CAMP in AV can also bring economic benefits.

Methodology: The abstract does not explicitly detail a methodology; rather, it outlines a review of existing short-term (housing, nutrition) and long-term (genetics, health) strategies, especially nutritional interventions (altered roughage-to-concentrate ratios, fats, probiotics, antioxidants, electrolytes, minerals), to mitigate heat stress in ruminants.
Highlights: Heat stress negatively impacts ruminant production and farm economics; nutritional interventions like altered forage-to-concentrate ratios, supplemental fats, probiotics, antioxidants, electrolytes, and mineral mixtures can help mitigate these effects and sustain productivity.

Paper Title: Evaluating the impact of climate change on yield and water use efficiency of different dry-season rice varieties cultivated under conventional and alternate wetting and drying conditions
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced rice yield (CAR15, Sen Kra Ob), decreased harvest index (HI), decreased water use efficiency (WUE), superior WUE in AWD compared to CF.

Drivers of Risk: Increased temperature (Tmin, Tmax), variable rainfall, heat stress impact on longer-duration rice varieties (CAR15, Sen Kra Ob).
Methodology: Rice cultivation under alternate wetting and drying (AWD) and continuous flooding (CF) was assessed using the AquaCrop model, calibrated and validated with 2023 dry season field experiments; IPCC's SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios from CMIP6 were used to project rice growth from 2040-2070.
Highlights: The study assesses rice cultivation under AWD and CF using IPCC scenarios, showing AquaCrop's reliable simulation of climate impact on rice, with longer growing varieties suffering significant yield reductions under heat stress, while AWD proves more water-efficient and drought-adaptive.
More Notes: Rice cultivation under future climate scenarios (SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5) shows increased temperatures and rainfall, negatively impacting longer-duration rice varieties with reduced yield, harvest index, and water use efficiency, while alternate wetting and drying (AWD) demonstrates superior water use efficiency as a potential drought adaptation strategy.
Adaptation considered: AWD is assessed as a drought-adaptive strategy for rice cultivation, demonstrating superior water use efficiency compared to CF. The analysis incorporates future scenarios (SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5) to evaluate adaptation effectiveness against projected heat stress impacts on different rice varieties.

Drivers of Risk: Seasonality, climate change, nonstationarity of supply and demand, neglecting climatic influences on demand, MWS sensitivity to demand changes.
Methodology: A calibrated systems model of a real-world municipal water system (MWS) was used to investigate the impact of climate-sensitive vs. climate-insensitive demand modeling on system vulnerability indicators (exceedance of historical daily mean imported water).
Highlights: Ignoring climate impacts on municipal water system demands leads to overestimations of imported water volume (up to 50%) and misclassifies vulnerabilities, while climate-sensitive demand models significantly reduce error and improve vulnerability categorization; the system is more sensitive to demand changes than supply changes, highlighting the need to accurately model both for effective climate change impact assessments.
More Notes: Econometric models often used for municipal water system vulnerability assessments undervalue climate's influence on water demand, leading to overestimations of imported water needs and misclassification of vulnerabilities, highlighting the necessity of climate-sensitive demand modeling for accurate assessments.
Adaptation considered: The paper focuses on how neglecting climatic influences on water demands in models used for municipal water system (MWS) vulnerability assessments leads to inaccurate results. It highlights the importance of climate-sensitive demand estimates for correctly categorizing vulnerabilities and improving model accuracy. The paper suggests future research should examine the coupled influences of modeled supply and demand accuracy on MWS performance. Adaptation is implicitly considered by advocating for climate-sensitive demand modeling to better understand and manage MWS vulnerabilities in the face of a changing climate, thereby informing adaptation strategies.

Drivers of Risk: GHG emissions, global warming, rising sea levels, hotter seas, increased droughts and floods, acidification.
Methodology: The review synthesizes existing research to outline the interaction between global warming and human influence on coastlines, emphasizing the significance of long-term monitoring, ecological indicators, and understanding aquatic biodiversity and ecosystem functioning in response to global warming.
Highlights: The abstract highlights the critical threats climate change poses to marine ecosystems, including habitat loss, species range shifts, and increased disease prevalence, and underscores the importance of long-term ecological monitoring and the development of indicators to understand and mitigate these changes.
More Notes: Climate change, driven by greenhouse gas emissions, is severely impacting aquatic ecosystems globally, leading to unprecedented conditions not seen in millions of years, causing declines in ocean productivity, shifts in food web dynamics, loss of habitat-forming species, altered species ranges, and increased disease prevalence, highlighting the urgent need for long-term monitoring, ecological indicators, and understanding of aquatic biodiversity's response to global warming.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly mentioned in the abstract. The focus is on impacts, monitoring, understanding responses, and mitigation roles of aquatic systems, not on adaptive strategies.

In this respect, complementary measures targeting hydration, work-rest regimes, ventilated garments, self-pacing, and mechanization can be adopted to protect outdoor workers. Category: Health > Heat and Cold (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, etc.)
Methodology: A systematic review was conducted using Scopus, Web of Science, PubMed, and Google Scholar to identify relevant studies on climate change and heat-stress control measures among outdoor workers, screening 2001 articles and ultimately including 29 eligible papers for data extraction, covering worker populations across 14 countries.
Highlights: Global warming increases extreme heat events, threatening outdoor workers' health; resilience can be enhanced through adaptation strategies like acclimatization, shading, optimized clothing, planned breaks, and hydration, with a need for improved awareness, training programs, and policies in tropical and subtropical regions.
More Notes: Outdoor workers' heat resilience can be improved by integrating climate change adaptation into occupational health programs and implementing strategies such as acclimatization, shading, optimized clothing, planned breaks, hydration, work-rest regimes, ventilated garments, self-pacing, and mechanization.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is mentioned through heat mitigation strategies (acclimatization, shading, optimized clothing, breaks), integration of climate change adaptation into occupational health programs, and complementary measures targeting hydration, work-rest regimes, ventilated garments, self-pacing, and mechanization. The abstract also underscores a lack of awareness regarding certain adaptation strategies and interventions. It calls for implementing risk management strategies, adaptive measures, heat risk awareness, practical interventions, training programs, and protective policies to boost tolerance and resilience. Cost-benefit evaluations of thermal stress adaptation measures are also suggested.

Paper Title: Systemic effects of heat stress on poultry performances, transcriptomics, epigenetics and metabolomics, along with potential mitigation strategies
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced poultry performance, disrupted physiological state, altered cellular mechanisms & transcriptomics, compromised digestive/immune/circulatory/neuroendocrine systems, changes in transcriptomics/metabolomics/epigenomics.

These ROS/RNS produced due to heat stress alter the underlying cellular mechanisms and transcriptomics. Methodology: The review article elucidates the systemic effect of heat stress on poultry physiology, performances, transcriptomic, epigenetics and metabolomics, along with potential mitigation strategies for the poultry industry.
Highlights: Heat stress, exacerbated by global warming, negatively impacts broiler chicken physiology, performance, and underlying cellular mechanisms (transcriptomics, metabolomics, epigenomics), affecting digestive, immune, circulatory, and neuroendocrine systems; effective and sustainable mitigation strategies are needed.

Paper Title: The environmental factors affecting solar photovoltaic output
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced PV efficiency and output; physical damage to PV systems.
sourcematerial: First, solar irradiance has strong geographic and temporal variability, making it the most significant factor. Second, raising module temperature reduces efficiency by 0.4–0.5 % per degree Celsius, limiting productivity in hotter climates. Third, atmospheric conditions (clouds, aerosols, pollutants, and dust) can reduce electricity output by up to 60 %, especially in desert regions. Fourth, terrain factors like albedo and snow present mixed effects, with increased reflection boosting output but snow obstructing panels. Fifth, extreme weather like wildfires and hailstorms cause substantial damage, while solar eclipses lead to large but short-lived output losses. Finally, long-term changes in solar irradiance, driven by climate change and air pollutants, present future challenges for maintaining PV efficiency.; Page 2: The drivers of risk include solar irradiance, ambient temperature, atmospheric conditions (clouds, aerosols, pollutants, and dust), terrain effects (albedo, snow), extreme weather events (wildfires, hailstorms, solar eclipses), and long-term irradiance changes driven by climate change and air pollutants. Drivers of Risk: Solar irradiance, temperature, atmospheric conditions (clouds, aerosols, dust, pollutants), terrain (albedo, snow), extreme weather (wildfires, hailstorms, eclipses), long-term irradiance changes, humidity, wind.
Methodology: Methodology includes: STC and PTC standards; daily average capacity factor over 30 years; APE (ratio of integrated irradiance to photon flux density); module operating temperature (heat generation/loss balance); I-V and P-V curve analysis at different temperatures; statistical analysis of temperature coefficients; Nominal Operating Cell Temperature (NOCT); cleaning method classification (mechanical, self-cleaning, electrostatic, natural) based on dust particle size; self-cleaning coating types (hydrophobic, super-hydrophilic); global maps of average albedo in summer/winter (measurements averaged over June-August and December-February 2014-2024).
Highlights: Hurricanes can disrupt power generation, but resilient systems like Babcock Ranch maintained power during Hurricane Ian; fresh snow has a high albedo, reflecting a large percentage of solar irradiance; wind can increase efficiency by cooling modules during peak sunlight hours; cleaning is a major operating expense in desert regions, but can increase PV capacity factors by 50–100 %; technologies that monitor and remove impurities using wave motion can be effective; electrostatic cleaning is suitable in regions facing water scarcity.
More Notes: Heat production by solar PV farms can raise the surrounding temperature. Aerosol optical thickness impacts solar efficiency. Dust can reduce large-angle reflections, increasing captured radiation. Increased humidity cools modules, enhancing efficiency. Edge placement protects interior modules from wind stress; similar effects are provided by fences. High albedo increases solar insolation and energy generation. Wildfires' aerosols reduce PV performance dramatically.
Adaptation considered: Cleaning methods (mechanical, self-cleaning, electrostatic, natural) are considered to mitigate dust accumulation. Module temperature management, including leveraging wind for cooling, is discussed. Geographic and temporal variability are addressed.

Drivers of Risk: Solar irradiance, temperature, atmospheric conditions (clouds, aerosols, pollutants, dust), terrain effects (albedo, snow), extreme weather (wildfires, hailstorms, solar eclipses, hurricanes), long-term irradiance changes (climate change, air pollutants), wind, humidity.
Methodology: Methodology: Parameters are measured using STC and PTC standards. Daily average capacity factor is summarized over 30 years. APE is the ratio of integrated irradiance to photon flux density. Module operating temperature is determined by the balance of heat generation and heat loss to the environment. Analysis of I-V and P-V curves at different temperatures and statistical analysis of temperature coefficients. Nominal Operating Cell Temperature (NOCT) is used. Cleaning methods are classified as mechanical, self-cleaning, electrostatic, and natural. Global maps of average albedo in summer and winter months use measurements averaged over June, July, August 2014–2024 for summer, December, January, February 2014–2024 for winter. Self-cleaning coatings include hydrophobic and super-hydrophilic types.
Highlights: Hurricanes can disrupt power generation, but resilient systems can maintain power; wind can increase efficiency by cooling modules; fresh snow has a high albedo boosting irradiance; technologies monitor and remove impurities using wave motion, electrostatic cleaning is suitable for water-scarce regions.
More Notes: Geographical variation in global horizontal irradiance (GHI) is shown in Fig. 4 and the effect of irradiance intensities on solar cell I-V and P-V curves is shown in Fig. 5; Heat production by solar PV farms can raise the surrounding temperature; Global map of aerosol optical thickness averaged over January 2019 to December 2023 is shown in Fig. 13; Dust reduces large-angle reflections from the cover glass, potentially increasing captured radiation, and increased humidity improves a module’s efficiency by lowering its temperature; Modules at edges shelter those at the interior, reducing wind stress, dependent on array spacing, and fences provide a similar sheltering effect; Increased reflection from higher albedo surfaces increases the insolation incident on solar modules, leading to increased energy generation; extreme wildfires in September 2020 reduced solar energy production by 30% in California.
Adaptation considered: Cleaning methods (mechanical, self-cleaning, electrostatic, natural) are considered to mitigate dust accumulation. Module temperature management via wind and humidity, and array spacing for wind stress reduction are also mentioned.

Outcome(s) of Interest: Adaptive diversification, ecosystem productivity, biodiversity, reduced inputs, environmental quality, human wellbeing, sustainable farming, ecosystem health

Drivers of Risk: Climate change, biodiversity loss, environmental variability, marginal environments, conventional monoculture methods, reduction of inputs/environmental load, trade-off between productivity and biodiversity.
Methodology: Field experiments in Japan and Sub-Saharan Africa assessed Synecoculture's agroecosystems (biodiversity, productivity, soil health) without conventional inputs; big data and AI (open-source database, drone image analysis, 3D modeling) managed ecological complexity; ecological data analysis monitored seasonal dynamics and plant adaptive strategies; theoretical modeling established the Integrated Model of Physiological and Ecological Optima (IMPEO); health impact studies evaluated health benefits via metabolome and biomarker analysis; socio-economic assessments measured economic impacts in Africa and participatory studies in Japan enhanced biodiversity and mitigated risks.
Highlights: Synecoculture offers transformative food production by enhancing biodiversity and productivity with minimal inputs, demonstrating improved ecosystem health, economic benefits, and positive human health outcomes, thereby addressing climate change and biodiversity loss challenges.
More Notes: Synecoculture, an ecological farming approach, demonstrates improved biodiversity, overall productivity, soil health, and reduced environmental impact compared to conventional monoculture through field experiments in Japan and Sub-Saharan Africa, integrating big data, AI, and ecological data analysis; it also exhibits health benefits via metabolome analysis and clinical assessments, socio-economic benefits in the Global South, and educational potential in urban settings, suggesting a sustainable farming model that enhances both human and ecosystem health.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Synecoculture is presented as an adaptation strategy to climate change and biodiversity loss. It leverages diverse, mixed-species agroecosystems to autonomously adapt to environmental variability. The approach aims to enhance productivity and biodiversity while reducing inputs and environmental impact, thereby improving human wellbeing and ecosystem health in both rural and urban settings.

Paper Title: Accounting for forest fire risks: global insights for climate change mitigation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Forest fire risk premia size, impact on carbon offset prices, spatial/temporal variation, and importance for offset programs.

Forest fire risks will increase with climate change in some locations...combining economic datasets and global remote sensing data from 2001 to 2020...forest fire risk premia better account for the risk of forest burning...large spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires across countries between 2001 and 2020...incorporating forest fire risk premia into carbon credits/offset programs...heterogeneity in fire risk premia across 10 Canadian provinces."; The paper focuses on forest fire risks, carbon sequestration and storage in forests, and the impact of fire risk on carbon credit/offset programs.; Combining economic datasets and global remote sensing data from 2001 to 2020. Drivers of Risk: Fire risk, climate change, forest loss, economic datasets, remote sensing data, forest fire risk premia, carbon credits, offsets, spatial heterogeneity, inter-temporal heterogeneity
Methodology: The study combined economic datasets and global remote sensing data from 2001 to 2020 to quantify individual country forest fire risks and estimate forest fire risk premia.
Highlights: Forest fire risk premia can exceed historical burned areas, significantly impact carbon credit/offset break-even prices in certain countries, exhibit substantial spatial and temporal heterogeneity, and necessitate proper incorporation into carbon credit/offset programs, as highlighted by varying risk premia across Canadian provinces.
More Notes: Forest fire risk premia, accounting for the risk of burning in carbon offset programs, are often larger than historical burn areas, significantly impact carbon credit pricing in some countries, exhibit spatial and temporal heterogeneity, and are crucial for proper incorporation into carbon credit/offset programs, as shown by the analysis of fire risk premia across 10 Canadian provinces.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in the abstract. The focus is on quantifying forest fire risk and its implications for carbon offset pricing, not on adaptation strategies to reduce fire risk or its impacts.

Paper Title: Global socioeconomic risk assessment of rainstorms based on different CMIP6 scenarios in the future
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased socioeconomic risk from rainstorms globally, highest under SSP5-8.5, lowest under SSP1-2.6; greatest risk in Asia, least in Europe; risk in North & South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania tied to socioeconomic exposure; risk in Europe tied to rainstorm indicators; SSP5-8.5 has largest uncertainty, ARDs also have large uncertainty.
sourcematerial: Adaptation considered: Climate features: Economic dynamics: Impact interactions: Space disaggregation: Impact persistence: Hazard definition: More Notes: Highlights: Methodology: Approach: Category: Drivers of Risk: Outcome(s) of Interest: (1) The socioeconomic risk from rainstorms in most regions of the world will increase in the future, with the largest increase in the SSP5-8.5 scenario and the smallest increase in the SSP1-2.6 scenario. (2) Overall, in the two future periods, the least socioeconomic risk from rainstorms is in Europe, and the largest socioeconomic risk from rainstorms is in Asia, especially the southern and southeastern parts of Asia, which have both the highest mean socioeconomic risk and the largest growth of socioeconomic risk. (3) The socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in North and South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania are more closely related to socioeconomic exposure changes, while the socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in Europe are more closely related to the three rainstorm indicators. (4) Among the three scenarios, the SSP5-8.5 scenario has the largest uncertainty, and among the three rainstorm indicators, ARDs have the largest uncertainty.; "The socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in North and South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania are more closely related to socioeconomic exposure changes, while the socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in Europe are more closely related to the three rainstorm indicators."; The study calculated three rainstorm indicators (annual rainstorm days, ARDs; annual rainstorm intensity, ARI; and annual rainstorm maximum, ARM), combined them into an index to indicate rainstorm hazard, ... and finally combined the rainstorm hazard index and socioeconomic exposure index to calculate the socioeconomic risk index of rainstorms. Drivers of Risk: Socioeconomic exposure changes (North & South America, Africa, Asia, Oceania); Rainstorm indicators (Europe).
Methodology: Rainstorm hazard was calculated using an index combining annual rainstorm days (ARDs), intensity (ARI), and maximum (ARM) derived from 22 GCMs (CMIP6) under SSP1-2.6, 2-4.5, and 5-8.5. A socioeconomic exposure index was calculated using projected population and GDP data from SSP1, SSP2, and SSP3. Rainstorm hazard and socioeconomic exposure indices were combined to derive a socioeconomic risk index for rainstorms, compared across time periods (1995–2014, 2041–2060, 2081–2100) globally and continentally. Uncertainty in rainstorm indicators was also analyzed.
Highlights: Global socioeconomic risk from rainstorms will increase in most regions, especially Asia, under all SSP scenarios, with SSP5-8.5 showing the largest increase; risk changes in North/South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania are driven by socioeconomic exposure, while in Europe they are driven by rainstorm indicators; SSP5-8.5 and ARDs have the largest uncertainty, concentrated in central North America, Central America, central and southern Africa, central South America, eastern Asia, and southern Oceania.
More Notes: Globally, socioeconomic risk from rainstorms will likely increase, especially under high emissions scenarios, with Asia facing the highest risk and Europe the lowest; risk changes are driven by socioeconomic exposure in most continents but by rainstorm indicators in Europe; uncertainty is highest under SSP5-8.5 and for annual rainstorm days, particularly in central North America, Central America, central and southern Africa, central South America, eastern Asia, and southern Oceania.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this study. The research focuses on quantifying socioeconomic risk from rainstorms based on climate model projections and socioeconomic pathways, without incorporating adaptive measures that could reduce that risk.

Methodology: The paper reviews existing studies on heat stress (HS) and biotic stress interactions in plants, examining their physiological and molecular effects, combined impact on crop growth and yields, and recent advances in developing strategies to address multi-stress scenarios.

Paper Title: Root zone soil moisture in over 25% of global land permanently beyond pre-industrial variability as early as 2050 without climate policy
Outcome(s) of Interest: Permanent soil moisture departures from pre-industrial variability; regional variations (drying: Central America, southern Africa, Mediterranean, South America; wetting: southeastern South America, northern Africa, southern Asia); increasing severity with warming; larger area affected over time; mitigation key.

Drivers of Risk: Root zone soil moisture, CMIP6 models, SSP scenarios, regional warming, evaporative demand, precipitation changes, water balance terms, ECS values, anthropogenic warming, land system changes, model uncertainties, soil moisture variability, permanent departures, hydrological changes.
Methodology: Uses CMIP6 output from 14 ESMs to assess future deviations in root zone soil moisture from a pre-industrial PiControl baseline across four SSPs (2021-2100). Evaluates permanent departures in 43 IPCC climate reference regions, analyzing monthly and yearly data. Considers model spread without weighting based on performance or interdependence, using Python for data processing.
Highlights: Root zone soil moisture departures act as an early warning for regions that are projected to have a permanently different water cycle in the coming decades; High ECS models could result in more pronounced soil moisture departures; the soil moisture in regions with permanent wet departures is expected to be permanently higher; the difference in the seasonal mean total soil moisture content of SSP1–2.6 and SSP3–7.0 from the PiControl scenario in 2100, and percentage of models in SSP1–2.6 and SSP3–7.0 that show a permanent departure from the PiControl baseline in different seasons in 2100.
More Notes: The study investigates changes in root zone soil moisture using a CMIP6 multi-model ensemble, basing analyses on IPCC WGI climate reference regions to avoid downscaling issues and improve regional interpretations. It highlights variability in spatial patterns of soil moisture changes, particularly with warmer scenarios exhibiting increased drying and wetting contrasts. There are noted inconsistencies in model predictions across neighboring regions, with a focus on model limitations and uncertainties due to misrepresented land-atmosphere interactions. Emission trajectories significantly influence regional drying and wetting patterns. Despite substantial mitigation, a considerable portion of the land might permanently deviate from pre-industrial soil moisture levels by 2100, underscoring the need for regional adaptation strategies.
Adaptation considered: Brief mention on page 12 of land cover adaptation. Page 16 has a call for swift adaptation to new hydrological situation in several regions even with global mitigation efforts.

Drivers of Risk: Climate change, plant pathogens, emergence of new diseases, reliance on agrochemicals.
Methodology: Innovative molecular detection tools, remote sensing, and biological practices are highlighted as methodologies for fast diagnosis, long-term monitoring of plant health, and ecofriendly disease management.
Highlights: Plant pathogens significantly threaten global food security by causing pre- and post-harvest crop losses, exacerbated by climate change and new diseases; innovative detection tools and biological control practices offer sustainable alternatives to environmentally harmful agrochemicals for improved plant disease management and enhanced crop production.
More Notes: Climate change exacerbates threats to food security by increasing plant disease incidence and severity, necessitating innovative and sustainable disease management strategies to safeguard agricultural productivity.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is implicitly considered through the discussion of innovative molecular detection tools, remote sensing for monitoring plant health, and biological practices for plant disease management as alternatives to agrochemicals. These methods are presented as ways to improve food security in the face of climate change and emerging diseases. The extent to which adaptation is considered is limited, focusing primarily on technological and biological solutions for managing plant diseases.

Methodology: Crop conditions were assessed using phenologically corrected Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI2) from 2018-2022 Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel (HLS) data, aligned based on crop emergence dates derived from the WISE algorithm; the resulting baseline was compared to other years, also considering growth degree days (GDD), and validated against NASS Crop Progress & Condition (CPC) data.
Highlights: Phenologically corrected vegetation index (EVI2) aligns multi-year satellite data based on crop emergence dates (derived from Landsat/Sentinel-2) and growth degree days (GDD) to create a baseline for normal crop condition, enabling improved within-season crop condition monitoring and yield prediction, validated against NASS Crop Progress & Condition data.
More Notes: Phenologically corrected vegetation index time series, aligned using crop emergence dates and growth degree days, improve crop condition assessment and yield prediction compared to calendar-day averaging, validated against NASS Crop Progress & Condition data.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this abstract. The focus is on improving crop condition assessment using phenological corrections to account for yearly variations in planting dates and climate, aiming for better monitoring and yield prediction, but not explicitly addressing adaptation strategies.

More Notes: Heat stress mitigation strategies in poultry include selective breeding for heat-tolerant lines, nutritional interventions (vitamin E, betaine, resveratrol, antioxidants), improved housing, and precision farming technologies to address negative impacts on feed intake, water homeostasis, gut integrity, and meat quality.
Adaptation considered: Experiments investigate physiological and performance impacts of heat stress on broiler chickens. Adaptation strategies are not explicitly mentioned in the provided extracts.

Methodology: The study developed a deep learning U-Net model to predict near real-time urban flood extents, utilizing Sentinel-1 imagery, daily rainfall, and spatial data (including slope, hydrologic soil group, and imperviousness) from 2014 to 2023 in Miami-Dade County, Florida.

Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this paper. The focus is on flood extent prediction using deep learning, not on adaptive measures.

This study highlights the design of a vehicle with an 80 kW fuel cell and a 2.64 kWh lithium-ion battery, simulated to refuel 4.34 kg of hydrogen in 280s, enabling a travel distance of 650 km. The findings demonstrate that FCEVs are competitive with conventional vehicles regarding range and refueling time, while offering significant environmental benefits, especially when utilizing green hydrogen. Drivers of Risk: GHG emissions, carbon emissions, air pollution, increased emissions from battery production, energy-intensive tire production (carbon black), local energy grid composition, infrastructure sustainability, petroleum product demand.
Methodology: Systematic review methodology included: PRISMA 2020 guidelines; review of 88 peer-reviewed articles (2019-2024); rigorous screening/exclusion criteria; manual title/abstract screening by two independent reviewers using Excel; data extraction to assess environment, organizations, technology, and policy impacts; bibliometric analysis with VOSviewer for co-occurrence maps; keyword analysis to identify themes/trends; co-authorship country analysis to visualize global collaborations; lifecycle assessments (LCAs) to evaluate environmental performance (raw material extraction to end-of-life); and modeling tools like AIMSUN for energy efficiency assessment, while prioritizing studies with robust methodologies to mitigate bias, but lacking a formal risk of bias assessment.
Highlights: EV adoption significantly reduces GHG emissions, improves air quality, promotes energy savings, fosters technological innovation, creates jobs, and reshapes the energy sector, especially when coupled with renewable energy, efficient battery recycling, and smart charging strategies.
More Notes: The co-occurrence maps identify key themes related to EVs, including energy consumption, environmental impact, and traditional automotive technologies, with the US being a central node in international authorship collaborations; the need for policy frameworks for electric vehicle adoption and sustainable transport strategies is emphasized, as well as the need for continued innovation in charging strategies and strategic management of EV infrastructure to ensure efficiency and grid reliability; further research is called for in long-term sustainability of EVs, technological integration with smart grids, economic analyses, behavioral studies, policy evolution, and global comparative studies.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered:
Adaptation is considered through:

*   Adaptive strategies for energy efficiency, like optimized scheduling and dynamic pricing.
*   Adjusting to contextual factors like route diversity and seasonal variations impacting energy consumption.
*   Developing region-specific EV technologies adaptable to various environmental conditions.
*   Innovative routing algorithms integrating battery health considerations.
*   PV-powered charging systems with hybrid modulated filter compensation to maximize energy utilization under varying PV conditions.

Drivers of Risk: EV manufacturing emissions, carbon-intensive batteries, grid composition influence, petroleum demand reduction, battery recycling impact, tire production emissions, contextual energy consumption factors, policy framework criticality.
Methodology: The study is a systematic review of 88 peer-reviewed articles, following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, focusing on EV adoption from 2019-2024. It used rigorous screening and exclusion criteria, manual screening of titles/abstracts by two independent reviewers, data extraction using Excel, and bibliometric analysis using VOSviewer to create co-occurrence maps. The analysis included keyword analysis, lifecycle assessments (LCAs) to evaluate environmental performance, and digital simulations to validate systems. While no formal risk of bias assessment was performed, studies with robust methodologies were prioritized.
Highlights: EVs significantly reduce GHG emissions and improve air quality, with lifecycle emissions lower than ICEVs, especially when using renewable energy; battery recycling and innovative tire materials further reduce the carbon footprint.
More Notes: EV adoption research encompasses energy consumption, environmental impact, and automotive technologies; the US is a central node in international co-authorship networks.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation considered through optimized scheduling and dynamic pricing for EV fleets, modelling route diversity and seasonal variations to improve energy efficiency, and developing region-specific solutions for challenging climates. Battery swapping, routing algorithms considering battery health, and predictive models for energy needs of electric buses are also adaptation strategies.

Adaptation considered: The abstract considers adaptation through improved management and monitoring tools for power companies and first responders to handle power-line-sparked wildfires on a large scale. This includes a proposed continually updating algorithm designed to pinpoint high-risk areas within power line corridors and a remote sensing mission to provide inputs for the algorithm. The intent is to better inform energy companies and provide warnings to early responders.

Paper Title: Agrivoltaics for small ruminants: A review
Outcome(s) of Interest: Potential mutual benefits: vegetation management (sheep), microclimate improvement for animal welfare (PV arrays), positive soil impacts (moisture retention, vegetation growth), improved urination distribution (shading), positive environmental performance, low material/carbon footprint, short payback periods.
sourcematerial: Adaptation considered: Climate features: Economic dynamics: Impact interactions: Space disaggregation: Impact persistence: Hazard definition: More Notes: Highlights: Methodology: Approach: Category: Drivers of Risk: Outcome(s) of Interest: The sheep aid solar farm operators with maintenance aspects, specifically vegetation management and associated costs. The PV arrays influence the microclimate conditions on the farm in terms of wind speed (and direction), temperature and humidity, including rain distribution, providing for better animal welfare in terms of heat stress and protection against harsh weather – especially in the face of climate change. Once operational, the soil is positively impacted with better moisture retention and vegetation growth improves in hotter months. The widespread shading also means (potentially) a better distribution of urination events with less overloading on the land and lower leachate... Overall, the available evidence suggests that the environmental performance of agrivoltaics is positive. Apart from the impact on the land, the life cycle of the systems have low material and carbon footprints, with short carbon and energy payback periods...; The construction process does have a negative impact on the soil, but the land can be restored in a short period of time.; The mitigation of climate change will require the large-scale uptake of renewable energy generation, in particular wind and solar. Utility-scale solar photovoltaics (PVs), or solar farms, will necessitate the transformation of agricultural land. Agrivoltaics is the dual land-usage solution with the integration of solar PV arrays with ongoing agricultural production. The farming of ruminants, especially sheep, is particularly suited for agrivoltaics in that little modification of the infrastructure is required to accommodate sheep grazing beneath and between the PV arrays. However, many uncertainties remain and this review paper provides more insights into the technology, the role of small ruminants, the potential impacts on agriculture productivity and the land, as well as other sustainability aspects of agrivoltaics. Previous and ongoing research suggests there are (potentially) mutual benefits for both operations to occur on the same land – if the integration is designed well.; This review paper provides more insights into the technology, the role of small ruminants, the potential impacts on agriculture productivity and the land, as well as other sustainability aspects of agrivoltaics. Drivers of Risk: Construction impact on soil.
Methodology: This is a review paper providing insights into the technology, the role of small ruminants, and potential impacts of agrivoltaics on agricultural productivity, the land, and sustainability aspects, based on previous and ongoing research.
Highlights: Agrivoltaics, integrating solar PV arrays with agriculture (specifically sheep grazing), offers mutual benefits: sheep provide vegetation management for solar farms, while PV arrays improve microclimate conditions for animal welfare. Soil impacts are initially negative but improve post-construction with better moisture retention and vegetation growth. Overall environmental performance is positive with low material and carbon footprints.
More Notes: Agrivoltaics, integrating solar PV with sheep farming, offers potential mutual benefits: sheep manage vegetation, solar arrays improve microclimate for animal welfare, soil benefits from moisture retention and vegetation growth after initial construction impacts, and systems have low life cycle footprints, though further quantification across climates is needed.
Adaptation considered: Agrivoltaics, by integrating solar PV with agriculture (specifically sheep farming), is presented as an adaptation strategy. The PV arrays modify the microclimate, potentially benefiting animal welfare by reducing heat stress and providing protection from harsh weather under climate change.

Paper Title: Enhancing Global Food Security Opportunities for the American Meteorological Society
Outcome(s) of Interest: Food security, health impacts, migration, national security, sustainable development, engagement, technology gaps, research improvement.
sourcematerial:
"food insecurity, negatively impacts health and well-being, drives mass migration, and undermines national security and global sustainable development...the opportunities for the AMS community to address food security...AMS community is well-positioned to scale up its engagement across the global food system to address existing scientific needs and technology gaps to improve global food security."; "Interactions between weather, climate, and the food system are identified as key factors influencing food security.", "Extreme weather and climate events, such as droughts, cold snaps, hurricanes, and other severe weather events, are impacting food security.", "The paper identifies extreme weather events like droughts, floods, heat waves, cold spells, and storms as impacting food security.", "Mentions climate changes at plant-to-field scales impacting irrigation and plant breeding.", "The primary drivers of risk discussed are anomalies in precipitation, temperature, and soil moisture, as well as climate variability modes like ENSO and the Indian Ocean Dipole.", "Meteorological and climate impacts, extreme climate events, anthropogenic climate change", "Droughts, heat waves, cascading climate-related events, poverty, rapid urbanization, weak governance, and the decline of ecosystems are impacting outcomes.", "Weather and climate shocks, such as droughts, impact food security and sustainable development, particularly in vulnerable communities relying on rainfed agriculture.", "Systemic risks are interdependent failures across a system that might lead to cascading events or a breakdown of the entire system. Recent methodological advancements have dramatically improved the ability to detect and attribute the signature of anthropogenic climate change in extreme climate events.", "Cascading climate-related events, poverty, rapid urbanization, weak governance, and the decline of ecosystems are threatening food security, increasing disaster risk, and, at times, introducing new threats."; Food security is a key pillar of environmental security yet remains one of the world’s greatest challenges. ...Ensuring food security is a delicate balance of myriad concerns within the atmospheric and Earth sciences, agronomy and agriculture engineering, social sciences, economics, monitoring, and policymaking. ...the interactions between weather, climate, and the food system and how they influence food security...the opportunities for the AMS community to address food security.; The methodology involves monitoring key Earth system variables, interpreting forecasts at different time horizons (6-9 months before, within 6 months of, and within 3 months of a growing season), and leveraging forecasts of opportunity based on climate phenomena.

Uses combinations of crop models, land surface schemes, hydrologic parameterizations, data-driven models, and historical bias-adjusted climate datasets. Development of historical counterfactual climate datasets is needed for direct detection and attribution.

The analysis involves the use of tailored climate forecasts and vulnerability analyses to trigger anticipatory actions for drought preparedness, such as developing soil and water conservation structures and introducing drought-resistant seeds.

For example, forecast-based financing is a form of humanitarian action that uses weather and climate forecasts and detailed risk analyses to release financial resources ahead of a disaster in order to enable early action.
Drivers of Risk: Weather variability, climate phenomena, extreme events, precipitation anomalies, temperature anomalies, soil moisture, climate variability modes, ENSO, Indian Ocean Dipole, droughts, floods, heat waves, cold spells, hurricanes, systemic risks, poverty, urbanization, weak governance, ecosystem decline, food security vulnerabilities, cascading climate events, multibreadbasket failures.
Methodology: The methodology involves: 1) monitoring hydrometeorological, climatological, and agronomic data across various time and space scales; 2) interpreting forecasts at different time horizons (6-9 months before, within 6 months of, and within 3 months of a growing season); 3) leveraging forecasts of opportunity based on climate phenomena; 4) integrating climate forecasts with socioeconomic data; 5) engaging stakeholders to improve food security decision-support tools (co-production, O2R/R2O); 6) using testbeds for O2R and R2O knowledge transfer with iterative development and verification (Agile); 7) employing combinations of crop models, land surface schemes, hydrologic parameterizations, data-driven models, and historical bias-adjusted climate datasets; 8) developing historical counterfactual climate datasets for attribution; 9) using tailored climate forecasts and vulnerability analyses to trigger anticipatory actions (e.g., soil and water conservation, drought-resistant seeds); 10) ensuring data is timely, relevant, accessible, accurate, usable, and useful; and 11) incorporating climate change information into agriculture and food security.
Highlights: The AMS community plays a key role in addressing food security through scaled-up engagement, interdisciplinary approaches, actionable information from climate monitoring, improved communication/co-production, attribution of climate change impacts, and leveraging forecast-based financing to mitigate risks and build resilience.
More Notes: Efforts to integrate climate change information into agriculture, especially seasonal-to-interannual variability, are long-standing; AMS needs to better link discussions on modeling and attribution of climate impacts to food security applications and encourage collaboration across sectors; analysis of compound events (droughts, wildfires, heat waves) and multibreadbasket failures, along with foresight scenarios and early warnings, are increasingly important.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered:
Adaptation is considered through:
*   Integration of climate forecasts with socioeconomic data.
*   Stakeholder engagement for decision-support tools.
*   'Co-production' of research addressing real-world challenges (e.g., EDDI).
*   Use of testbeds for knowledge transfer and iterative development.
*   Tailored climate forecasts triggering anticipatory actions like drought-resistant seeds and water conservation.
*   Forecast-based financing for early action ahead of disasters.

Paper Title: Enhancing Global Food Security
Outcome(s) of Interest: Improved global food security through AMS community engagement.
sourcematerial:

Drivers of Risk: Extreme weather events, climate change (precipitation, temperature, soil moisture anomalies), climate variability modes (ENSO, Indian Ocean Dipole), cascading climate-related events, poverty, rapid urbanization, weak governance, ecosystem decline.
Methodology: Methodology includes: monitoring Earth system variables, interpreting forecasts at different time horizons (6-9 months before, within 6 months of, and within 3 months of a growing season), leveraging forecasts of opportunity based on climate phenomena, integrating climate forecasts with socioeconomic data, engaging stakeholders in co-production (O2R and R2O), using testbeds for knowledge transfer and iterative development/verification, employing combinations of crop models, land surface schemes, hydrologic parameterizations, data-driven models, historical bias-adjusted climate datasets, and tailored climate forecasts with vulnerability analyses to trigger anticipatory actions.
Highlights: The AMS community should scale up engagement in the food system; address scientific and technology gaps; combat food insecurity due to extreme weather; improve communication via co-production, prototypes, and testbeds; match models to decision-making needs; leverage climate predictability; adopt FEWS NET's stakeholder model; improve attribution of climate change in extreme events; manage systemic risks like drought; and utilize forecast-based financing.
More Notes: Integration of climate change information into agriculture, especially seasonal-to-interannual variability, is crucial; the AMS needs to better link discussions and modeling to food security applications and encourage cross-sector collaboration; analysis of compound events (droughts, wildfires, heat waves) and multibreadbasket failures, along with foresight scenarios and early warnings, are essential.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is addressed through integrating climate forecasts with socioeconomic data, stakeholder engagement to improve food security decision-support tools, co-production of knowledge, testbeds for knowledge transfer, and tailored climate forecasts to trigger anticipatory actions (e.g., drought-resistant seeds, soil and water conservation). Forecast-based financing is also used to enable early action.

Paper Title: Tracing Urban Drinking Water Sources: Global State of the Art and Insights From an IAEA-Coordinated Research Project
Outcome(s) of Interest: Better assessment, mapping, and management of urban drinking water resources. Enhanced drinking water treatment strategies and improved urban water management and conservation plans.

The latter is reflected in the spatial isotope snapshot variability. Similarly, while d-excess was fairly close to the global mean value (+10‰) across all urban centres (10‰–15‰), reservoir-based drinking water systems show lower values (up to ~ −20‰) (Arlington, TX, USA and Gaborone, Botswana), as a result of strong evapoconcentration processes. δ18O time series and depth-integrated sampling highlighted the influence of the catchment damping ratio in the ultimate intake water composition.; Climate change, inter-annual precipitation variability, recurrent droughts and flash flooding, coupled with increasing water needs, ... Similarly, while d-excess was fairly close to the global mean value (+10‰) across all urban centres (10‰–15‰), reservoir-based drinking water systems show lower values (up to ~ −20‰) (Arlington, TX, USA and Gaborone, Botswana), as a result of strong evapoconcentration processes. δ18O time series and depth-integrated sampling highlighted the influence of the catchment damping ratio in the ultimate intake water composition. Drivers of Risk: Climate change, inter-annual precipitation variability, recurrent droughts, flash flooding, increasing water needs.
Methodology: The study utilizes isotope hydrology to evaluate urban drinking water systems, focusing on water source mapping and temporal isotope variability, employing high-resolution sampling techniques to understand environmental and climatic impacts on water supply.
Highlights: Urban areas rely on diverse water recharge sources (mountains, groundwater, distant basins), reflected in spatial isotope variability; high-resolution isotope monitoring reveals diurnal variations tied to source type; reservoir-based systems exhibit lower d-excess due to evapoconcentration; catchment damping influences intake water composition; new traceable tools can improve water treatment and urban water management under global warming.
More Notes: Advancements in isotope techniques enable precise assessment and management of urban drinking water sources, revealing varied diurnal isotope variations and emphasizing the need for enhanced water treatment and conservation strategies amid climate change impacts.
Adaptation considered: The abstract discusses the adaptation of urban drinking water systems through the integration of advanced water isotope methodologies. It underlines the adaptation through improved assessment and mapping of water resources to manage drinking water supply efficiently. The study provides new tools and insights, emphasizing the importance of enhanced water treatment strategies and management plans to address variability in water resources linked to climate changes, supporting both local and broader water distribution challenges.

Paper Title: Simulating synergistic effects of climate change and conservation practices on greenhouse gas emissions and crop growth in long-term maize cropping systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased CO2 & N2O emissions; increased GWP; decreased maize yield & total biomass; increased SOC; residue retention w/ tillage increases CO2 & N2O.

Drivers of Risk: Temperature, precipitation, CO2 concentration (increased CO2 emissions, decreased yield).
Methodology: Future climate conditions (2065-2084) for a Nebraska maize cropping system were projected using 12 RCMs-GCMs combinations under RCP8.5 and elevated CO2. The RZWQM2 model simulated the effects of four conservation practices under these climate projections, with temperature, precipitation, and CO2 concentration identified as key drivers.
Highlights: Future climate change (RCP8.5) in Nebraska is projected to increase CO2 and N2O emissions, leading to a rise in global warming potential, while decreasing maize yield and total biomass despite a small increase in soil organic carbon; residue retention with tillage is predicted to exacerbate CO2 and N2O emissions compared to residue removal with no-till.
More Notes: Future climate change (RCP8.5) in Nebraska is projected to increase CO2 and N2O emissions, leading to a higher global warming potential, despite a slight SOC increase; elevated CO2 levels offer limited photosynthetic benefits, with rising temperatures shortening growth cycles and ultimately decreasing maize yield and total biomass; residue retention with tillage is predicted to worsen CO2 and N2O emissions compared to residue removal with no-till.
Adaptation considered: The study simulates the effects of four long-term conservation practices under future climate scenarios, assessing their impact on GHG emissions, crop yield, and SOC. These practices can be considered forms of adaptation, although the abstract focuses on comparing their effectiveness rather than explicitly framing them as adaptation strategies.

Paper Title: A macro-level analysis of the socio-economic impacts of climate change driven water scarcity: Incorporating behavioural and resilience aspects
Outcome(s) of Interest: Socio-economic impacts; macroeconomic impacts; sector-specific impacts (agriculture, food, electricity); competitiveness effects (some sectors benefit); water use catch-up effect.
sourcematerial:
"socio-economic impacts at a macro-level of temporary water scarcity," "Sector-specific analyses shed light on the potential negative impacts of water scarcity on sectors like agriculture, food, and electricity production and distribution," "certain sectors can benefit from competitiveness effects, which can mitigate the adverse economic implications of water scarcity," "these sectors may contribute to a catch-up effect on water use."; Page 1: "Water scarcity is driven by factors such as climate change, insufficient rainfall, or drought, which impact sectors including agriculture, food, and electricity production."; Page 5: "The drivers of risk are temporal water scarcity events (Scenarios 1 and 2)."; Page 6: "Month-long water scarcity events are assumed, lasting the entirety of May in a given year. Concurring water scarcity events are assumed to occur in both May and August (also lasting the entire month), with the latter being more severe."; Page 9: "The primary driver of risk is reduced freshwater availability impacting agriculture, chemical and pharmaceutical manufacturing, and electricity generation."; Recognising the urgent need to address water scarcity resulting from climate change...this paper utilises a custom-built dynamic multisectoral model to assess the socio-economic impacts at a macro-level of temporary water scarcity...Sector-specific analyses shed light on the potential negative impacts of water scarcity on sectors like agriculture, food, and electricity production and distribution.; Page 1: The analysis uses a dynamic multisectoral model incorporating behavioural and resilience aspects to assess the broader economic implications of water scarcity. Page 2: The analysis uses the DEMACRO-ESS model, a general equilibrium model that evaluates how different investment behaviours (perfect foresight, myopic expectations, and imperfect foresight) impact the macro-economy during and after water scarcity. Page 4: Uses a custom built dynamic multi-sectoral CGE model, called DEMACRO-ESS, parameterized on a 2020 Social Accounting Matrix (SAM) for Germany at monthly frequency. Page 6: A computable general equilibrium (CGE) model named AMOS is used to simulate the economic impacts of water scarcity, by reducing the water efficiency parameter in the model.; The analysis uses the DEMACRO-ESS model, a general equilibrium model that evaluates how different investment behaviours (perfect foresight, myopic expectations, and imperfect foresight) impact the macro-economy during and after water scarcity. (Page 2)
The analysis is performed using DEMACRO-ESS simulation and considers different investment behavior models (Myopic, Imperfect foresight, and Perfect foresight). The model uses multi-level production functions and includes water and land as natural capital. The model adopts a Constant Elasticity of Substitution (CES) technology with substitution elasticities equal to 0.3. (Page 5)
A computable general equilibrium (CGE) model named AMOS is used to simulate the economic impacts of water scarcity, by reducing the water efficiency parameter in the model. (Page 6)
A sensitivity analysis using the DEMARCO-ESS model is conducted with different Armington trade elasticities to account for varying levels of economic openness. (Page 7)
Methodology is detailed in: "The analysis presented in this paper is based on a custom built dynamic multi-sectoral CGE model, called DEMACRO-ESS. The model is based on the AMOS CGE modelling framework and incorporates (neo) Keynesian characteristics. A full list of equations of the base models is given in Lecca et al. (2013) and Ross et al. (2023). Drivers of Risk: Temporal water scarcity events (May, August); drought conditions; reduced freshwater availability.
Methodology: The study employs the DEMACRO-ESS, a dynamic multi-sectoral computable general equilibrium model based on a 2020 Social Accounting Matrix for Germany, to simulate the macroeconomic impacts of water scarcity under different investment behavior scenarios: myopic, imperfect foresight, and perfect foresight. The model incorporates multi-level production functions using a Constant Elasticity of Substitution technology and emphasizes resilience aspects by including water and land as natural capital. Simulations consider various assumptions about investment foresight and economic openness, with sensitivity analysis conducted using different Armington trade elasticities.
Highlights: Firms' foresight affects economic resilience to water scarcity; agriculture is most negatively impacted, while some sectors see competitiveness gains, and economic openness decreases GDP decline but worsens employment impacts.
More Notes: The analysis highlights how foresight in firms influences economic response to water scarcity, with sectors facing both negative and mitigating competitive impacts, with potential increased water use over time.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through investment behaviors (myopic, imperfect foresight, perfect foresight) in response to water scarcity. These behaviors impact economic resilience and are modeled within a dynamic, multisectoral CGE framework (DEMACRO-ESS) that includes water and land as natural capital. Scenarios of water scarcity are simulated, and the model assesses the macroeconomic and sectoral impacts under different levels of investment foresight, essentially evaluating adaptive capacity. Sensitivity analyses also explore how economic openness (trade elasticities) affects these impacts and adaptation.

Paper Title: Increasing irrigation-triggered landslide activity caused by intensive farming in deserts on three continents
Outcome(s) of Interest: Negative impacts on local communities; continuous movement for at least 7 years; lateral expansion and longitudinal retrogression of headscarp; transformation into landslide clusters; increased frequency and disaster-causing ability with population growth and agricultural intensification.

An increase in irrigation-triggered landslides (ITL) in dry climates has negative impacts on local communities. Findings show that the most severe surface deformation of ITL occurs on the landslide body. The ITL displacement curve indicates the ITL will maintain continuous movement for at least 7 years. The headscarp of ITL shows lateral expansion and longitudinal retrogression, transforming landslides into landslide clusters. Here, we use Multi-Temporal Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (MT-InSAR), vectorization, and differential method to study surface deformation, ground displacement, and changes in headscarp morphology and topography in regions prone to ITL, aiming to uncover the evolution and spatiotemporal distribution of ITL. Findings show that the most severe surface deformation of ITL occurs on the landslide body. Meanwhile, the ITL displacement curve indicates the ITL will maintain continuous movement for at least 7 years, while ancient ITL also poses a threat. Moreover, the headscarp of ITL shows lateral expansion and longitudinal retrogression on the horizontal ground, whereby the scale of expansion is greater than that of retrogression, which transforms landslides into landslide clusters. Finally, the topographic changes further reveal that the main development pattern of ITL is lateral expansion. We suggest that the frequency and disaster-causing ability of ITL will increase greatly with further population growth and related intensification in the agricultural sector. © 2024 The Authors; Population growth and agricultural intensification lead to stress on landscapes that are highly sensitive to land-use changes.
