Regional cropping systems are dominated by corn (Zea mays)–soybean (Glycine max) rotations managed with conventional tillage, industry-average rates of fertilizer and pesticide inputs uniformly applied, few cover crops, and little animal integration. In 2020, KBS LTAR initiated the Aspirational Cropping System Experiment as part of the LTAR Common Experiment, a co-production model wherein stakeholders and researchers collaborate to advance transformative change in agriculture. The Aspirational (ASP) cropping system treatment, designed by a team of agronomists, farmers, scientists, and other stakeholders, is a five-crop rotation of corn, soybean, winter wheat (Triticum aestivum), winter canola (Brassicus napus), and a diverse forage mix. All phases are managed with continuous no-till, variable rate fertilizer inputs, and integrated pest management to provide benefits related to economic returns, water quality, greenhouse gas mitigation, soil health, biodiversity, and social well-being. Cover crops follow corn and winter wheat, with fall-planted crops in the rotation providing winter cover in other years. The experiment is replicated with all rotation phases at both the plot and field scales and with perennial prairie strips in consistently low-producing areas of ASP fields. The prevailing practice (or Business as usual [BAU]) treatment mirrors regional prevailing practices as revealed by farmer surveys. Stakeholders and researchers evaluate the success of the ASP and BAU systems annually and implement management changes on a 5-year cycle.; The Kellogg Biological Station Long-term Agroecosystem Research site (KBS LTAR) ... initiated the Aspirational Cropping System Experiment as part of the LTAR Common Experiment ... Methodology: The MaxEnt-SPAM approach, coupling socioeconomic factors and climate change scenarios (RCP2.6-SSP1, RCP4.5-SSP2, and RCP8.5-SSP3), was used to predict global wheat cultivation distribution in the near-term (2011–2040), mid-term (2041–2070), and long-term (2071–2100).
Highlights: Coupling socioeconomic factors with climate change predictions, the study anticipates shifts in global wheat cultivation: increases in the Southern Hemisphere, declines in the Northern Hemisphere, and an 80% predictive accuracy, highlighting significant interactions affecting future distribution patterns and productivity.

Paper Title: Hydroponics: Exploring innovative sustainable technologies and applications across crop production, with Emphasis on potato mini-tuber cultivation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced dependency on agricultural land and pesticides; mitigates negative effects of extreme weather events; potential solution to insufficient access to high-quality potato seed stock; increased tuber production; better yield and crop productivity; saving water, energy, and space; alternate choice for modern sustainable agriculture.

Hydroponics, a soilless cultivation system, gaining interest as it reduces the dependency on agricultural land, and pesticides, and can be implemented in areas with poor soil quality, thus mitigating the negative effects of extreme weather events. Potato is an essential dietary staple crop grown throughout the world and is a major source of food security in underdeveloped countries. However, due to the climatic changes, it is predicted that a significant loss in the suitability of land for potato production would occur, thus leading to potato yield loss. Recently, many case studies have emerged to highlight the advancement of agricultural hydroponic systems that provide a promising solution to the massive production of potato mini tuber at high efficiency. This review paper evaluates popular hydroponic methods and demonstrates how hydroponic has emerged as the go-to, long-term, sustainable answer to the perennial problem of insufficient access to high-quality potato seed stock. The paper discusses the research and innovation possibilities (such as artificial intelligence, nanoparticles, and plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria) that potentially increase tuber production per plant under optimal hydroponic growth circumstances. These approaches are examined considering new scientific discoveries and practical applications. Furthermore, it emphasizes that by enduring significant reforms in soilless food production systems (particularly for potatoes), the food supply of a rapidly growing population can be addressed. Since hydroponics systems are productive and easily automated without soil and optimal environmental conditions, future hydroponics farming is promising. In conclusion, the hydroponics system provides better yield and crop productivity by saving water, energy, and space. Drivers of Risk: Climate change impacts on traditional potato farming; drought; unpredictable weather; contaminated water; nutrient imbalances in hydroponics; technical failures in hydroponic systems (root rot, algal growth); vulnerability of hydroponic systems to nutrient distribution, water pump, or nozzle clogging failures.
Methodology: Hydroponic potato cultivation methods include ebb and flow, deep water culture (DWC) using plastic boxes, glass basins, ice boxes, or fish ponds with air pumps, and nutrient film technique (NFT) using thin layers of nutrient solution in tanks or polyvinyl chloride trays. Aeroponics, where roots are suspended in air and sprayed with nutrient solution, is also used. Studies have evaluated electrical conductivity (EC) and planting densities for optimal nutrient intake and growth of different potato cultivars, and compared tuber production across different methods and substrates.
Highlights: Hydroponics, particularly aeroponics and NFT systems, offers a climate-resilient alternative to traditional potato farming, addressing food supply issues and land limitations by providing disease-free mini-tubers and potentially higher yields with efficient use of resources; specific methods like wick systems (optimally with eight wicks for 'Dejima' potatoes), ebb and flow, and DWC also show promise, with aeroponics demonstrating significantly increased tuber production compared to conventional methods.
More Notes: Hydroponics and aeroponics offer climate-resilient alternatives to traditional agriculture, enabling crop production in challenging environments, including urban settings and areas with poor soil quality; smart agriculture technologies like AI and nanoparticles may enhance soilless cultivation systems; potential risks include failures in nutrient distribution or water supply; aeroponics can support potato cultivation irrespective of the agroecosystem and can significantly increase mini-tuber production compared to conventional methods; plant density in some aeroponic systems is approximately 24 plantlets/m2, yielding roughly 150 mini-tubers.
Adaptation considered: Hydroponic and aeroponic systems (including Deep Water Culture, Nutrient Film Technique) are examined as alternative potato cultivation methods, with focuses on optimizing nutrient delivery, electrical conductivity, and planting densities. These systems are explored to increase tuber production, especially in challenging environments, and to reduce water usage.

Drivers of Risk: climate change, land suitability loss, yield loss; drought, unpredictable weather, contaminated water (traditional agriculture); nutrient imbalances (hydroponics); root rot, technical failure, algal growth (ebb and flow); system failure (hydroponics).
Methodology: Various hydroponic methods (ebb and flow, deep water culture (DWC), nutrient film technique (NFT), aeroponics) are used for potato seed production, utilizing plastic boxes, glass basins, ice boxes, or fish ponds for DWC, and polyvinyl chloride trays for NFT; studies assess electrical conductivity and planting densities for different potato cultivars, and compare tuber production in NFT vs traditional methods; aeroponics increased potato mini tuber output up to 8.5 times more than conventional cultivation.
Highlights: Hydroponics offers a climate-resilient alternative to traditional potato farming, mitigating extreme weather impacts and land limitations; soilless systems provide disease-free mini tubers and potentially higher yields. Aeroponics requires less water and produces significantly more tubers per plant, especially for certain cultivars, showing promise for space and marine applications. Different hydroponic methods vary in efficiency, with NFT showing positive results for minituber production.
More Notes: The paper focuses on hydroponics and aeroponics as innovative agricultural systems that can enhance food security by enabling efficient crop production, including potato mini-tubers, in challenging environments independent of soil conditions, highlighting technologies like AI and aeroponics but does not directly address broader climate impacts pertinent to the U.S. National Climate Assessment.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation approaches highlighted include innovative cultivation techniques like deep-water culture (DWC), nutrient film techniques (NFT), and aeroponics, which are considered particularly for their potential in challenging environments. These methods enable crop production in areas with limited fertile land or harsh conditions, such as deserts and cold terrains. Improved water and nutrient efficiency alongside increased yield, especially in aeroponics, are noted as benefits, addressing challenges from conventional farming due to climate impacts. However, technical challenges and maintenance requirements remain limitations.

Paper Title: Population at risk of dengue virus transmission has increased due to coupled climate factors and population growth
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased global population in areas with high dengue climate suitability; expansion of suitable areas in North America, East Asia, Mediterranean.
sourcematerial:
"We found an expansion in climate suitability in North America, East Asia and the Mediterranean basin...Globally, we estimated that the population in areas with high climate suitability has grown by approximately 2.5 billion."; "The increased risk of dengue transmission is driven by changes in climate, particularly temperature and humidity, along with urbanization and population growth." "Climate-dependent variables, specifically temperature and humidity, are drivers of dengue transmission risk." "The increase in the global population living in areas with high climate suitability for DENV transmission emphasizes the impact of population growth and urbanization in high-risk regions, particularly the Global South."; Dengue virus transmission has increased over the last four decades seemingly due to changes in climate, urbanization and population growth...we examined how shifts in these climatic variables and human population growth have contributed to the change in the geographical distribution and size of the global population living in areas with high climate suitability from 1979 to 2022.; Mechanistic and statistical (species distribution modeling) approaches are used to estimate dengue transmission potential based on climate variables. Analysis uses Index P based on temperature and humidity data, evaluates trends from 1979 to 2022, and displays results using spatiotemporal maps. Long-term trends in climate suitability are quantified using a seasonal Mann–Kendall test. The study uses Tobit models to estimate relationships between climate suitability (Index P) and DENV transmission, with a meta-analysis performed for country-level estimates.

Drivers of Risk: Climate change (temperature, humidity), population growth, urbanization.
Methodology: Climate suitability for dengue transmission was estimated using mechanistic and statistical (species distribution modeling) approaches, focusing on temperature and humidity data from 1979-2022; trends were evaluated using Index P and a seasonal Mann-Kendall test, considering population, income, and climate zone categorizations, and Tobit models were used with a meta-analysis for country-level estimates, employing a pre-whitening algorithm to address autocorrelation.
Highlights: Expansion of climate suitability for dengue is occurring in North America, East Asia, and the Mediterranean basin; both land area and population with high climate suitability have increased, especially in tropical and subtropical regions; the global population in high-suitability areas has grown, driven by population growth in the Global South and climate change in the Global North; significant changes are observed in high-density areas and temperate climate zones.
More Notes: The study compares climate suitability and dengue incidence in specific regions and finds that population growth disproportionately occurred in areas with historically high climate suitability. Increases in climate suitability were observed in the southern United States and other global regions, while decreases were noted in northern Australia and other areas.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this study, which focuses on quantifying changes in climate suitability for dengue transmission based on historical climate data and socioeconomic factors.

Drivers of Risk: Climate change (temperature, humidity), population growth, urbanization.
Methodology: Dengue transmission potential was estimated using Index P based on temperature and humidity data from 1979-2022, with long-term trends quantified using a seasonal Mann-Kendall test, and Tobit models employed with a meta-analysis for country-level estimates, controlling for autocorrelation via pre-whitening; demographic, economic (World Bank income groups), and climate zone (Köppen-Geiger) data were also used to contextualize findings.
Highlights: Climate suitability for dengue transmission has expanded in North America, East Asia, and the Mediterranean basin; globally, both land area and population with high climate suitability have increased, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions, with population growth being a major driver, especially in the Global South, while temperate zones experienced the largest net change in climate suitability.
More Notes: The study compared climate suitability and dengue incidence across several countries and found that population growth disproportionately occurred in areas with historically high climate suitability; climate suitability increased in the southern U.S. and other regions while decreasing in others.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this analysis of dengue transmission potential. The study focuses on historical trends and climate suitability modeling without incorporating adaptive measures.

Drivers of Risk: Climate change; temperature; relative humidity; wind speed; variability of climatic parameters.
Methodology: An integrated framework is used to quantify the impact of climate change on steel corrosion rates by combining Global Climate Models (to project changes in temperature, relative humidity, and wind speed) with an analytical corrosion rate prediction model, enabling comparison of projected corrosion rates to historical conditions.
Highlights: Climate change impacts on steel corrosion rates vary regionally; increases are projected for Australia, North America, Europe, and especially oceans, while decreases are expected in Asia and Africa.
More Notes: The study highlights that climate change alters atmospheric factors such as temperature, relative humidity, and wind speed, impacting the corrosion rates of steel structures; these changes increase corrosion in regions like North America, Europe, and all oceans, particularly the Antarctic and Arctic, while reducing it in parts of Asia and Africa.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is implicitly considered through the development of a framework to model and predict corrosion rates under changing climate conditions. The integration of climate models and an analytical corrosion model suggests a proactive approach towards anticipating and managing the impacts on steel structures. However, the focus is on prediction and understanding rather than explicit adaptation strategies or measures. Different regional predictions imply a potential for region-specific adaptation insights or recommendations, though not directly addressed.

The assessment of risk was conducted using the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) framework, which incorporates hazard, exposure, and vulnerability as integral components. Additionally, the response was added as a core component to the flood risk framework. A Self-Organizing Map (SOM) was implemented to carry out unsupervised clustering, which captured the interactions within vulnerability and overall risk components. The results indicate that counties in the eastern and southern regions of the United States, along with those in the coastal areas, face a heightened risk. This is primarily due to a larger population and a more significant concentration of properties with high hazard scores. However, our analysis also showed that the counties in these regions have very robust responses, including structural, non-structural, and emergency measures, subduing the risk associated with flooding. We also compared our maps with the National Risk Index (NRI), revealing a reasonable correlation between the Social Vulnerability Index (SoVI) and our vulnerability assessment. A notable distinction between the SoVI and our proposed vulnerability framework was our inclusion of unique vulnerability indicators referring to social, ecological, economic, and health factors specific to flooding, suggesting that vulnerability can vary depending on the type of natural hazard. Drivers of Risk: Hazard scores (population & property concentration), vulnerability (social, ecological, economic, health factors specific to flooding).
Methodology: Flood risk was assessed at the county level using the IPCC framework (hazard, exposure, vulnerability) with the addition of 'response' as a core component; a Self-Organizing Map (SOM) was used for unsupervised clustering of vulnerability and overall risk.
Highlights: Flood risk assessment at the U.S. county level reveals heightened risk in eastern, southern, and coastal areas due to concentrated populations and properties with high hazard scores while robust local responses mitigate flood risk.
More Notes: The study highlights that eastern, southern, and coastal U.S. counties are at an increased flood risk due to high population and property concentration, yet possess strong responses that mitigate this risk, differing from the SoVI by incorporating varied vulnerability factors specific to flooding, and underscores the utility of a county-scale approach for informed local policymaking.
Adaptation considered: Response (adaptation) is included as a core component of the flood risk framework, specifically looking at structural, non-structural, and emergency measures. The analysis suggests that robust responses in high-risk areas (eastern, southern, and coastal U.S.) subdue the risk associated with flooding.

Paper Title: Projected response of algal blooms in global lakes to future climatic and land use changes: Machine learning approaches
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased algal blooms in lakes globally, particularly in Africa, Asia, and North America, worsening by the end of the century under higher forcing scenarios (SSP370 and SSP585); underdeveloped regions show greater sensitivity.

However, the Nutrient Utilization Efficiency (NUE) of urea stands around 30%, as a result of its easy and premature loss to the environment through leaching and atmospheric oxidation. Apart from the economic repercussions, urea leachates contribute to global warming, water pollution, and adverse health effects on both humans and animals in the long term. On the other hand, the Haber-Bosch process, used for commercial production of urea, is highly energy-intensive and no longer sustainable. Therefore, it is important to explore alternative approaches to improving the delivery efficiency of urea by manipulating the release of nitrogen to match the plant demand. Drivers of Risk: Urea inefficiency, leaching, atmospheric oxidation, Haber-Bosch process energy intensity, increasing global population & demand.
Methodology: The paper reviews the use of engineered nanomaterials, including nanoparticles, layered-nanostructures, and nanotubes, as carriers for the slow and precise delivery of urea to crops, aiming to improve nutrient utilization efficiency (NUE) and reduce environmental impacts.
Highlights: Urea fertilizer, while essential for crop yields, has low nutrient utilization efficiency, leading to environmental problems and unsustainable production; nanodelivery systems offer a promising solution for slow and precise nitrogen release, improving efficiency and reducing negative impacts.
More Notes: Urea fertilizer's low nitrogen utilization efficiency contributes to global warming, water pollution, and health problems, while its production is energy-intensive; nanodelivery systems are being explored to improve urea's efficiency and reduce environmental impacts.
Adaptation considered: Nanodelivery of urea fertilizer is explored as an adaptation strategy to improve nutrient utilization efficiency, reduce environmental impacts, and address the unsustainability of current urea production methods. The focus is on using engineered nanomaterials for slow and precise nitrogen delivery to crops.

Paper Title: LSTM networks provide efficient cyanobacterial blooms forecasting even with incomplete spatio-temporal data
Outcome(s) of Interest: Prediction accuracy, cyanobacterial bloom, Early Warning Systems, LSTM effectiveness, forecasting horizons, skill improvement, alarm level 10 µg PC/L

Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic activities, global warming, increased frequency, global distribution of cyanobacterial blooms.
Methodology: An effective Early Warning System (EWS) for forecasting cyanobacterial blooms leverages 6 years of incomplete high-frequency spatio-temporal multiparametric data, incorporating phycocyanin fluorescence as a proxy for cyanobacteria, with a probe agnostic replicable method for data pre-processing and the development of specific time series suitable for predictive modeling; six predictive models, including autoregressive and multivariate Linear Regression, Random Forest, and Long-Term Short-Term (LSTM) neural networks, were evaluated over seven forecasting horizons from 4 to 28 days with consistent improvement seen in the multivariate LSTM across metrics such as MSE, R2, MAPE, Accuracy, F1, Kappa, and a forecasting-specific skill metric.
Highlights: An Early Warning System (EWS) for cyanobacterial blooms was developed using machine learning models and high-frequency data, with a multivariate LSTM model demonstrating the best performance, achieving up to 90% accuracy in predicting phycocyanin alarm levels and showing effectiveness in forecasting blooms 16-28 days in advance.
More Notes: Cyanobacterial blooms, exacerbated by human activities and global warming, are increasingly frequent and widespread; a multivariate LSTM-based early warning system demonstrates promising results in forecasting these blooms up to 28 days in advance.
Adaptation considered: The paper proposes an early warning system (EWS) for cyanobacterial blooms. This EWS allows for timely implementation of management measures, which reduces the risks associated with these blooms. The development and comparison of predictive models are presented as part of this EWS.

Paper Title: The economic commitment of climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: 19% global income reduction by 2050 (independent of emissions), committed damages outweigh mitigation costs, losses in low-latitude/low-income regions.

Global projections of macroeconomic climate-change damages typically consider impacts from average annual and national temperatures over long time horizons1–6. Here we use recent empirical findings from more than 1,600 regions worldwide over the past 40 years to project sub-national damages from temperature and precipitation, including daily variability and extremes7,8. Using an empirical approach that provides a robust lower bound on the persistence of impacts on economic growth, we find that the world economy is committed to an income reduction of 19% within the next 26 years independent of future emission choices (relative to a baseline without climate impacts, likely range of 11–29% accounting for physical climate and empirical uncertainty).; The analysis uses fixed-effects panel regression models...fixed-effects distributed lag models with first-differenced climate variables...The analysis uses empirical models and historical interannual variability to estimate climate impacts on economic output...The analysis uses empirical models with moderating variables capped at the 95th percentile of historical data to limit out-of-sample extrapolation...panel regression models with a selection of fixed effects and time trends to isolate plausibly exogenous variation with which to maximize confidence in a causal interpretation of the effects of climate on economic growth rates.; Page 1: The analysis uses fixed-effects panel regression models on data from more than 1,600 regions worldwide over the past 40 years to project economic damage through mechanisms like daily temperature and precipitation variability. Following a wide range of climate econometric literature, we use panel regression models with a selection of fixed effects and time trends to isolate plausibly exogenous variation with which to maximize confidence in a causal interpretation of the effects of climate on economic growth rates... Drivers of Risk: Average temperature, temperature variability, precipitation (total, wet days, extremes).
Methodology: The analysis uses fixed-effects panel regression models on data from 1,600+ regions worldwide over 40 years, with first-differenced climate variables in distributed lag models (8-10 lags for temperature, ~4 for precipitation), incorporating region/yearly fixed effects and region-specific linear time trends, to project economic damage from temperature/precipitation changes (including daily variability and extremes) under SSP2-RCP2.6 and SSP5-RCP8.5 scenarios, employing a Monte Carlo procedure and block-bootstrap resampling to estimate uncertainty, population weighting, capping moderating variables at the 95th percentile of historical data, and considering interactions of climate variables with historical climatic conditions while acknowledging the lack of empirical evidence for the influence of the number of wet days on macroeconomic growth.
Highlights: Climate damages until 2049 are already committed, outweigh mitigation costs, and disproportionately impact vulnerable nations, with temperature changes being the primary driver, but variability and precipitation exacerbating the losses.
More Notes: The empirical study on climate impacts projects a significant permanent global income reduction of 19% over the next 26 years, driven by temperature and precipitation changes, using the SSP5-RCP8.5 scenario and extended climate data analysis. Robust methodologies, including Monte Carlo simulations and detailed lag analysis, reveal persisting impacts over decades. Data for replication is accessible via Zenodo, and climate change projections show substantial temperature increases and varied precipitation changes across global regions.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation considerations in the analysis are somewhat limited, as the focus is predominantly on projecting economic damages resulting from climate change without fully integrating adaptive measures. While the study employs comprehensive models to assess impacts of various climate variables and explores their statistical distribution and persistence, it does not extensively incorporate potential adaptation strategies that regions might employ to mitigate these effects. It does, however, highlight the uneven distribution of climate change impacts, noting that countries with fewer resources to adapt are expected to face greater damages. This implies an awareness of the significance of adaptation, though its explicit inclusion in projections remains minimal.

Paper Title: The economic commitment of climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: 19% global income reduction by 2050 (committed damages); predominant cause: average temperature changes; exacerbated by other climatic components (+50%); regional heterogeneity; losses in lower latitudes (lower emissions, lower income); benefits at very high latitudes.
sourcematerial:
Global projections of macroeconomic climate-change damages typically consider impacts from average annual and national temperatures over long time horizons. Here we use recent empirical findings from more than 1,600 regions worldwide over the past 40 years to project sub-national damages from temperature and precipitation, including daily variability and extremes. Using an empirical approach that provides a robust lower bound on the persistence of impacts on economic growth, we find that the world economy is committed to an income reduction of 19% within the next 26 years independent of future emission choices (relative to a baseline without climate impacts, likely range of 11-29% accounting for physical climate and empirical uncertainty). These damages already outweigh the mitigation costs required to limit global warming to 2 degrees C by sixfold over this near-term time frame and thereafter diverge strongly dependent on emission choices. Committed damages arise predominantly through changes in average temperature, but accounting for further climatic components raises estimates by approximately 50% and leads to stronger regional heterogeneity. Committed losses are projected for all regions except those at very high latitudes, at which reductions in temperature variability bring benefits. The largest losses are committed at lower latitudes in regions with lower cumulative historical emissions and lower present-day income.; Pages 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 12, 16, 18, 19: Changes in average temperature, temperature variability, total annual precipitation, number of wet days, and extreme daily rainfall are identified as drivers of economic impacts/risks.; the world economy is committed to an income reduction of 19% within the next 26years independent of future emission choices... Drivers of Risk: Average temperature, temperature variability, precipitation (total, wet days, extremes).
Methodology: The analysis projects sub-national economic damages from temperature and precipitation using fixed-effects panel regression models on data from over 1,600 regions worldwide over the past 40 years. The methodology incorporates fixed-effects distributed lag models with first-differenced climate variables, region and yearly fixed effects, and region-specific linear time trends. It accounts for historical interannual variability by detrending data and includes interactions of climate variables with historical average climatic conditions. Uncertainty is estimated using a Monte Carlo procedure that samples from climate models and empirical models. To limit out-of-sample extrapolation, moderating variables are capped at the 95th percentile of historical data. Projections use SSP2-RCP2.6 and SSP5-RCP8.5 scenarios, with future socio-economic data based on SSP2, and population weighting is applied. An incremental lag selection procedure is used with information criteria. Three empirical specifications are used, with a preferred specification avoiding assumptions of infinite persistence. Clustered standard errors by region are used.
Highlights: Highlights indicate that committed climate change damages globally outweigh 2°C mitigation costs, predominantly due to temperature rise, and harm regions with lower emissions and income most, whereas daily temperature variability significantly escalates projected economic impacts.
More Notes: Empirical models are robust to autocorrelation and multicollinearity, with lag selection determined by information criteria; data and code are available via Zenodo for replication; climate change projections (SSP5-RCP8.5) from 1979-2019 to 2035-2065 are expressed as a percentage of historical variability (1979-2019 standard deviation of detrended climate variables); baseline specification is not specified as growth or level effect.
Adaptation considered: Limited explicit adaptation. Empirical models include moderating variables, capped at historical levels, to limit out-of-sample extrapolation. Region-specific linear time trends, interactions of climate variables with historical averages, and varying lag structures account for some heterogeneity. Some analysis includes spatial spillovers. Population weighting considers unequal impacts.

PM2.5 is a great concern due to its adverse effects on public health. Previous studies have examined the health burden from fire-related PM2.5 for the historical period; however, future global mortality associated with fire-PM2.5, particularly under the coexisting impacts of climate and socioeconomic changes, is yet to be projected. Here, we estimated the mortality associated to fire-PM2.5 (fire-PM2.5 mortality) for the present period (2010s) and then projected the mortality for the 2050s and 2090s under 10 scenarios created by combining two Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP2.6 or 6.0) with five Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP1-5). We used the Li-Park fire model in combination with a chemical transport model and health risk assessment to calculate fire-PM2.5 mortality. For the present period, the estimated fire-PM2.5 mortality was 135 180 (105 403-163 738), which is 1.7 deaths per 100 000 population globally. When countries were grouped by income, the fire-PM2.5 mortality rate was the highest in the high-income country group and lowest in the lowest-income country group. For the 2050s and 2090s, the fire-PM2.5 mortality rate was projected to decrease under most scenarios because of decreases in fire emissions and baseline mortality rate. However, a scenario of high population growth and low technical development (SSP3) together with severe global warming (RCP6.0) would lead to an increase in the fire-PM2.5 mortality rate in the 2090s, particularly in the highest-income countries, due to increased fire under drier and warmer weather conditions. Drivers of Risk: Climate change (drier/warmer conditions), socioeconomic pathways (population growth, technical development), RCP scenarios (2.6/6.0), fire emissions, baseline mortality rate.
Methodology: The study estimated present (2010s) and projected future (2050s, 2090s) mortality associated with fire-related PM2.5 (fire-PM2.5 mortality) under 10 scenarios combining RCP2.6/6.0 with SSP1-5, utilizing the Li-Park fire model, a chemical transport model, and health risk assessment.
Highlights: Globally, fire-related PM2.5 mortality is currently 1.7 deaths per 100,000, highest in high-income countries; projections to the 2050s and 2090s generally show decreases due to reduced fire emissions and baseline mortality rates, but a scenario combining high population growth (SSP3) and severe warming (RCP6.0) could increase mortality, especially in high-income countries, necessitating adaptation efforts.
More Notes: Future global mortality from fire-related PM2.5 is projected to mostly decrease due to lower fire emissions and baseline mortality rates, except under a high population growth/low technical development scenario coupled with severe warming, where mortality rates, particularly in high-income countries, could increase due to increased fires driven by drier and warmer conditions, indicating a need for adaptation.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation needs are mentioned only in the context of high-income countries, where the SSP3/RCP6.0 scenario projects increased mortality. The study highlights the need for adaptation efforts to avoid future mortality increases.

This study investigates the influence of climate change on maize yield in the Mobile River Basin (MRB) in the southeastern United States using the Decision Support System for Agrotechnology Transfer (DSSAT) crop model. We use four climate models from Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) under two Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSPs) of SSP245 and SSP585 to represent future changes in solar radiation, precipitation and temperature. In this study, we simulate crop yields using climate data from the past (1985–2010), the experimental period (2011–2017), and future projections (2026–2050, 2050–2075, and 2076–2100). The simulated crop yields are compared to historical yields to evaluate the adaptation measures selected to mitigate the impact of future climate scenarios, assuming no effective adaptation measures or changes in farming practices. We evaluate several adaptation strategies including optimization of planting date, fertilizer application date, implementing supplemental irrigation and modification of fertilizer doses.; "The findings indicated that by end of the 21st century, maize yield will fall by 8.2 % (-842 kg·ha−1) and 16.4 % (-1684 kg·ha−1) under the SSP245 and SSP585 scenarios, respectively." Methodology: The study uses the DSSAT crop model with CMIP6 climate model data (SSP245 and SSP585) to simulate maize yields in the Mobile River Basin (MRB) across historical (1985-2010), experimental (2011-2017), and future (2026-2100) periods, then evaluates adaptation strategies like adjusted planting/fertilizer dates and supplemental irrigation.
Highlights: Maize yield in the Mobile River Basin (southeastern US) is projected to decline by 8.2% and 16.4% by the end of the 21st century under SSP245 and SSP585 scenarios, respectively; however, yield loss can be mitigated through adaptation strategies such as earlier planting and fertilization, and supplemental irrigation.

Adaptation considered: Adaptation strategies (planting date, fertilizer application, irrigation, fertilizer doses) are evaluated using crop modeling to mitigate climate change impacts on maize yield. Yields are compared to historical yields assuming no adaptation to evaluate the effectiveness of the selected strategies.

Paper Title: Biochar-integrated reactive filtration of wastewater for P removal and recovery, micropollutant catalytic oxidation, and negative CO2e: Life cycle assessment and techno-economic analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Carbon negativity, pollutant removal, phosphorus recovery, biochar-integrated reactive filtration, techno-economic analysis, carbon sequestration.
Drivers of Risk: BC dose rate, carbon accounting method, scale of treatment facility.
Methodology: Life cycle assessment (LCA) and techno-economic analysis (TEA) models, compliant with ISO standards, were developed to evaluate a tertiary wastewater treatment system employing biochar-integrated reactive filtration (RF), with and without iron-ozone catalytic oxidation (CatOx); LCA was performed on a 49.2 m3/day pilot system and a 1130 m3/day WRRF-installed system, both modeled with a biochar addition rate of 0.45 g/L, while TEA utilized Monte Carlo stochastic modeling to assess the cost of water treatment at different scales.
Highlights: The study evaluates a carbon-negative biochar-integrated tertiary wastewater treatment system with catalytic ozonation, demonstrating 90%-99% phosphorus and >90% micropollutant removal efficiency, with potential for cost-effective, large-scale applications, highlighting its role in reducing carbon footprints and achieving carbon neutrality in water treatment.
More Notes: Biochar-integrated reactive filtration systems for tertiary wastewater treatment can achieve carbon-negative operation while effectively removing phosphorus and micropollutants, potentially establishing carbon negativity as a standard for water treatment.
Adaptation considered: The study explores the potential of biochar-integrated reactive filtration to achieve carbon-negative water treatment, essentially adapting water treatment processes to mitigate climate change by sequestering carbon. It quantifies the carbon footprint reduction associated with the technology.

Rosneft Oil Company is an active participant in the exploration and development of the Arctic shelf of Russia, also considering the Northern sea route as a strategic transport corridor. Since 2012, Rosneft has been con-ducting expeditions to study ice and metocean conditions in its license areas here. The article presents coverage of environmental studies conducted by Rosneft Oil Company at its license areas in the Arctic Seas, which resulted in the collection one of the world's largest database on ice and metocean conditions in the Arctic seas. The obtained observational data are used in applied research and targeted innovation projects. As an example, the authors present the results of long-term climate forecasts of ice conditions in terms of parameters that can affect navigation along the Northern sea route up to 2050. Analysis of the calculation results shows the following changes in metocean and ice conditions along the Northern sea route in comparison with the present averages (2011–2022) over the next 30 years: a) increase in the ice-free period by 1,5–2 months (and more – in the south-western part of the Kara Sea); b) increase of about ~3 оС in the mean annual temperature; c) disappearance of drifting ice along the route in summer, retreat of its edge to the present minimum; d) increase in the area and longer existence of the ob-served polynyas. Methodology: Rosneft Oil Company has conducted expeditions since 2012 in its Arctic license areas to study ice and metocean conditions, resulting in a large database used in applied research and innovation projects to produce long-term climate forecasts of ice conditions affecting navigation along the Northern Sea Route up to 2050.
Highlights: The article details an increase in Arctic ice-free periods by 1.5-2 months, a ~3°C rise in mean annual temperatures, disappearance of drifting ice in summer, and increased polynyas along the Northern Sea Route over the next 30 years, informed by Rosneft's comprehensive observational data and long-term climate forecasts.
More Notes: Rosneft's Arctic studies project a longer ice-free period (1.5-2+ months), a 3°C temperature increase, summer ice disappearance, and expanded polynyas along the Northern Sea Route by 2050.
Adaptation considered: The study provides data (ice conditions, temperature changes, polynya extent) relevant for planning shipping and other economic activities along the Northern Sea Route. This implicitly informs adaptation strategies for these activities.

Drivers of Risk: Drought (pre- and post-heading), changing climatic conditions, seasonal precipitation variations.
Methodology: 156 bread wheat genotypes (landraces from Turkey, Iran, Afghanistan, and high-yielding modern varieties) were subjected to pre-heading drought, post-heading drought, long-term precipitation-based irrigation, and seasonal precipitation with supplemental irrigation under rain shelters. Grain yield, physio-morphological, and quality traits were evaluated under these conditions.
Highlights: Wheat landraces from Turkey, Iran, and Afghanistan, while yielding less than modern varieties under ideal conditions, exhibit lower yield loss under drought stress and possess valuable traits like higher tillering and protein content, making them a crucial resource for breeding climate-resilient wheat varieties adapted to future drought scenarios.
More Notes: Landraces of wheat from Turkey, Iran, and Afghanistan show potential for drought tolerance and could be incorporated into breeding programs to improve climate resilience in modern wheat varieties.
Adaptation considered: The study considers adaptation by evaluating landraces (traditional wheat varieties) known to have adapted to changing climates over long periods, comparing their performance under drought stress with modern varieties. Paper Title: INTERNATIONAL CONVERGENCE TOWARDS A CLIMATE-NEUTRAL ECONOMY: MODELING THE AGRICULTURAL SECTOR
Outcome(s) of Interest: Inertial/divergent trend of agricultural economy towards decarbonization; slowing progress towards "net zero"; EU accelerated climate convergence weakening; long-term decarbonization capacity key for global convergence.
sourcematerial: Adaptation considered: Climate features: Economic dynamics: Impact interactions: Space disaggregation: Impact persistence: Hazard definition: More Notes: Highlights: Methodology: Approach: Category: Drivers of Risk: Outcome(s) of Interest: The results of the study reveal an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation, which slows down the overall movement towards “net zero” due to the presence of clubs of lagging agricultural countries that increase emissions in violation of international agreements. The EU is the most prominent example of accelerated climate convergence, but markers of its weakening in recent years are identified due to the inability of economies to maintain the pace of decarbonisation caused by economic constraints, technological barriers, policy and regulatory issues, and misunderstandings of climate neutrality goals. It is shown that the long-term decarbonisation capacity of the agricultural sector is the key trigger for a country to make a positive contribution to the global convergence towards climate neutrality.; "The results of the study reveal an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation, which slows down the overall movement towards “net zero” due to the presence of clubs of lagging agricultural countries that increase emissions in violation of international agreements...EU...weakening in recent years are identified due to the inability of economies to maintain the pace of decarbonisation caused by economic constraints, technological barriers, policy and regulatory issues, and misunderstandings of climate neutrality goals."; This article aims to construct a comprehensive convergent model for assessing the global and EU’s progress, degree of consolidation and symmetry of agricultural economies towards climate neutrality in the context of key international green initiatives...This research employs both qualitative and quantitative analyses to assess convergence measures in the agricultural sector’s carbon emissions...The results of the study reveal an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation...The EU is the most prominent example of accelerated climate convergence...long-term decarbonisation capacity of the agricultural sector is the key trigger for a country to make a positive contribution to the global convergence towards climate neutrality...global convergence towards climate neutrality in agriculture, highlighting the importance of digital technologies and smart agriculture as significant factors in global convergence.; Methodology / approach. This research employs both qualitative and quantitative analyses to assess convergence measures in the agricultural sector’s carbon emissions. The quantitative component relies on the sigma and beta convergence models to evaluate international convergence in carbon emissions CO2 dynamics. Additionally, statistical indicators of variation, skewness, Gini and Theil indices were calculated for delineating global smoothness and the concentration of agricultural emissions.; "Methodology / approach. This research employs both qualitative and quantitative analyses to assess convergence measures in the agricultural sector’s carbon emissions. The quantitative component relies on the sigma and beta convergence models to evaluate international convergence in carbon emissions CO2 dynamics. The dimensions of modeling are as follows: global sample within 194 countries with reliable emissions data; European Union, focusing on convergence within the EU27 member states, the United Kingdom, and Ukraine. The time period covers open data from 1972–2022. The dynamics of sigma and beta convergence is determined for the points, namely UN Stockholm Conference (1972), UN Rio Conference (1992), Kyoto Protocol (entry into force in 2005), Paris Agreement (2015). Additionally, statistical indicators of variation, skewness, Gini and Theil indices were calculated for delineating global smoothness and the concentration of agricultural emissions."; The study reveals an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation... Factors in this process include proactive compliance with global climate agreements, technology sharing and cooperation, digitalisation and smart agriculture, and green financing and investment... Drivers of Risk: lagging agricultural countries, economic constraints, technological barriers, policy/regulatory issues, misunderstandings of climate neutrality goals, inability to maintain decarbonization pace.
Methodology: Employs qualitative and quantitative analyses, using sigma and beta convergence models to assess international convergence in agricultural sector carbon emissions (CO2) from 1972-2022 across 194 countries globally and within the EU (EU27, UK, Ukraine), considering key milestones like the UN Stockholm and Rio Conferences, Kyoto Protocol, and Paris Agreement; calculates statistical indicators (variation, skewness, Gini, Theil indices) to delineate global smoothness and concentration of agricultural emissions.
Highlights: Agricultural decarbonization is trending inertially and divergently, slowing progress toward "net zero" due to lagging countries; EU shows accelerated convergence but is weakening; long-term decarbonization capacity is key; pace of decarbonization is more critical than initial emissions level; proactive agreement compliance, technology sharing, digitalization, and green finance are crucial; highlights importance of digital technologies and smart agriculture.
More Notes: The agricultural sector shows an inertial and divergent trend towards decarbonization globally, with some countries increasing emissions despite international agreements; the EU demonstrates accelerated climate convergence but faces weakening due to economic and technological constraints; proactive compliance with climate agreements, technology sharing, and digital agriculture are key to global convergence towards climate neutrality in agriculture.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is implicitly acknowledged by emphasizing technology sharing, digitalization, smart agriculture, and green financing as key factors for achieving climate neutrality in agriculture. The paper addresses economic, technological, policy, and cooperative barriers, suggesting the need for stakeholder integration, strategic actions, and evaluation. However, the primary focus is on decarbonization, with adaptation represented through cooperative and technological advancement strategies.

Drivers of Risk: Water surface temperature, precipitation, lake geomorphology.
Methodology: A hierarchical Bayesian spatiotemporal model (Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation - INLA) was developed to forecast cyanobacterial harmful algal bloom (cyanoHAB) occurrence across 2192 U.S. lakes using weekly satellite-derived cyanobacteria abundance, water surface temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology as predictors; the model's performance was compared to machine learning and neural network models, with INLA demonstrating superior prediction accuracy.
Highlights: A spatiotemporal model using satellite data, water temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology accurately forecasts harmful algal blooms (cyanoHABs) across the U.S., outperforming machine learning and neural network models with 90% accuracy in predicting WHO Alert Level 1 exceedances.
More Notes: A spatiotemporal model using satellite data, temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology can forecast harmful algal blooms across the U.S. with 90% accuracy, outperforming machine learning and neural network models.
Adaptation considered: The forecasting approach facilitates proactive water management, helping public health managers identify and mitigate cyanoHAB risks. The use of satellite data and the INLA model enhances predictive capabilities, allowing for targeted interventions across varying climate zones. The model's robustness to data gaps and unbalanced sampling supports its adaptability to real-world conditions, indicating its utility for managing and adjusting to changing algal bloom patterns.

Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced land suitability for almond farming in California by end of century, earlier bloom times.

Highlights: Conservation agriculture shows an overall positive effect on soil health under long-term warming, increasing soil organic carbon, microbial biomass carbon, and wheat yields (9.3% increase over 8 years), resulting in a 21% average increase in soil health compared to conventional agriculture and maintaining similar crop production levels.
More Notes: Conservation agriculture can mitigate the negative impacts of climate warming on food security by improving soil health and sustaining crop yields, with long-term warming even enhancing soil health and wheat yields under conservation agriculture compared to conventional methods.
Adaptation considered: Conservation agriculture is presented as an adaptation strategy to mitigate the negative impacts of climate warming on soil health and crop yields. The study suggests that conservation agriculture can improve soil health and maintain crop production levels under long-term warming conditions, demonstrating its potential to enhance resilience to climate change.

Paper Title: Severe Convective Weather in the Central and Eastern United States: Present and Future
Outcome(s) of Interest: Intensified SCW activity (especially in early-middle spring), shift towards higher radar reflectivity, potential increase in SCW severity and resultant damage.
sourcematerial:
Results indicate that the retrospective simulation captures reasonably well the spatial distributions and seasonal variations of the observed SCW events, with an exception of an overestimate along the Atlantic and Gulf coast. In a warmer-moister future, most regions experience intensified SCW activity, most notably in the early-middle spring, with the largest percentage increase in the foothills and higher latitudes. In addition, a shift of simulated radar reflectivity toward higher values, in association with the significant thermodynamic environmental response to climatic warming, potentially increases the SCW severity and resultant damage.; "the potential change in SCW frequency and intensity in response to anthropogenic climate warming is highly uncertain"; "a future climate sensitivity simulation based on the perturbed reanalysis-derived boundary conditions with the CMIP5 ensemble-mean high-end emission scenario climate change"; "In a warmer-moister future, most regions experience intensified SCW activity"; "a shift of simulated radar reflectivity toward higher values, in association with the significant thermodynamic environmental response to climatic warming, potentially increases the SCW severity and resultant damage."; The continental United States is a global hotspot of severe thunderstorms and therefore is particularly vulnerable to social and economic damages from high-impact severe convective weather (SCW), such as tornadoes, thunderstorm winds, and large hail.; "This study investigates SCW in the Central and Eastern United States in spring and early summer for the current and future warmed climate using two multi-year continental-scale convection-permitting Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model simulations. The pair of simulations consist of a retrospective simulation, which downscales the ERA-Interim reanalysis during October 2000–September 2013, and a future climate sensitivity simulation based on the perturbed reanalysis-derived boundary conditions with the CMIP5 ensemble-mean high-end emission scenario climate change. A proxy based on composite reflectivity and updraft helicity threshold is applied to infer the simulated SCW occurrence."; The continental United States is a global hotspot of severe thunderstorms and therefore is particularly vulnerable to social and economic damages from high-impact severe convective weather (SCW)...our knowledge of the spatiotemporal climatology and variability of SCW occurrence is still lacking, and the potential change in SCW frequency and intensity in response to anthropogenic climate warming is highly uncertain...Results indicate that...most regions experience intensified SCW activity, most notably in the early-middle spring...a shift of simulated radar reflectivity toward higher values, in association with the significant thermodynamic environmental response to climatic warming, potentially increases the SCW severity and resultant damage.; "Results indicate that the retrospective simulation captures reasonably well the spatial distributions and seasonal variations of the observed SCW events... In a warmer-moister future, most regions experience intensified SCW activity, most notably in the early-middle spring, with the largest percentage increase in the foothills and higher latitudes. In addition, a shift of simulated radar reflectivity toward higher values, in association with the significant thermodynamic environmental response to climatic warming, potentially increases the SCW severity and resultant damage."; N/A; No relevant source material provided.; "The impacts of climate change are not borne equally across the United States, and regional differences are emerging in the nature and intensity of climate change impacts.";  ;  ; ===

===; "The adaptation strategies considered include managed retreat, accommodation, and protection...adaptation acknowledges the uncertainties."
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic climate warming; warmer-moister future; intensified SCW activity; shift of radar reflectivity to higher values; significant thermodynamic environmental response.
Methodology: Two multi-year continental-scale convection-permitting WRF model simulations were used: a retrospective simulation downscaling ERA-Interim reanalysis (Oct 2000-Sept 2013) and a future climate sensitivity simulation based on perturbed reanalysis with a CMIP5 high-end emission scenario; SCW occurrence was inferred using a proxy based on composite reflectivity and updraft helicity.
Highlights: The continental U.S., a hotspot for severe thunderstorms, faces increased vulnerability due to uncertainties in severe convective weather (SCW) occurrence linked to climate change; however, simulations suggest intensified SCW activity, especially in spring, with a notable increase in severity due to warmer, moister conditions.
More Notes: Climate change is projected to intensify severe convective weather (SCW) in the central and eastern U.S., particularly in early-middle spring and at higher latitudes, potentially increasing SCW severity and damage.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is mentioned as a necessary response to climate change impacts, particularly regarding infrastructure, water resources, and coastal zones. Specific strategies include managed retreat, accommodation, and protection measures. Recognition of the need for flexible and adaptive approaches to account for uncertainties and evolving conditions is present.

Drivers of Risk: Windstorms; critical infrastructure vulnerability; inadequate aerodynamic testing; underestimation of peak wind loads (small-scale testing); Reynolds number sensitivity (sharp corners).
Methodology: LSU WISE open-jet testing facility allows comprehensive evaluation of critical infrastructure components (buildings, PV solar panels, wind turbines, bridges, vegetation, power lines) under controlled wind conditions, overcoming limitations of wall-bounded and small-scale testing by producing complete turbulence at a large scale, thereby providing a more accurate representation of real-world conditions and eliminating the need for turbulence modelling corrections.
Highlights: The LSU WISE open-jet wind testing facility enhances structural resilience under windstorms by providing accurate, large-scale simulations of real-world conditions; key findings include that integrating PV panels on gable roofs may not require additional reinforcement, open-jet testing produces higher peak pressures than small-scale testing, and wind loads on structures with sharp corners *are* sensitive to Reynolds number.
More Notes: The LSU WISE open-jet facility enhances structural resilience to windstorms by accurately testing critical infrastructure components like buildings, solar panels, and power lines under realistic wind conditions, leading to improved designs and a more sustainable built environment; findings challenge existing assumptions about wind loads, particularly on high-rise buildings and structures with sharp corners, demonstrating the superiority of large-scale open-jet testing for resilient structural design.
Adaptation considered: The facility aims to enhance structural resilience under windstorms, enabling the design and engineering of robust, climate-resilient systems. Design optimization and cost-effective installation methods are mentioned for building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Open-jet testing helps create resilient structures by providing more accurate real-world wind condition representation.

Paper Title: Heatwaves and occupational injuries and illnesses risk varied at localised spatial scale: A national study in Australia
Outcome(s) of Interest: Occupational injuries and illnesses (OI).
sourcematerial: This study, for the first time, addresses critical gaps in the existing literature by examining the impact of HWs on occupational injuries and illnesses (OI) at a fine spatial scale and projecting future OI burdens under climate change scenarios. The study reveals a substantial cumulative OI risk ranging between 5.1 and 29.0 % during heatwaves, with outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas showing heightened vulnerability. By utilising high-resolution geospatial data and future projections, our study provides new insights into the spatial heterogeneity of HW impacts on OI within cities, a previously underexplored area of research.; Younger workers, males, injury-related claims, and workers in areas with higher temperatures and less vegetation were identified as particularly susceptible to HWs. ...outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas showing heightened vulnerability.; This study, for the first time, addresses critical gaps in the existing literature by examining the impact of HWs on occupational injuries and illnesses (OI) at a fine spatial scale and projecting future OI burdens under climate change scenarios.; No source material provided.; "Using time series study design and distributed lag nonlinear models we establish the association between HWs, assessed via the Excess Heat Factor (EHF), and OI in Adelaide, Brisbane, Melbourne, and Sydney at the Statistical Area Level 3 (SA3)." ... "By utilising high-resolution geospatial data and future projections, our study provides new insights into the spatial heterogeneity of HW impacts on OI within cities..." ... "Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI..."; Heatwaves (HWs) pose significant risks to occupational safety...the impact of HWs on occupational injuries and illnesses (OI)...The study reveals a substantial cumulative OI risk ranging between 5.1 and 29.0 % during heatwaves, with outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas showing heightened vulnerability. Younger workers, males, injury-related claims, and workers in areas with higher temperatures and less vegetation were identified as particularly susceptible to HWs...Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI...These findings emphasise the importance of developing localized, climate-related adaptation and intervention strategies to safeguard workers.; "Heatwaves (HWs) pose significant risks to occupational safety in Australia... the impact of HWs on occupational injuries and illnesses (OI) at a fine spatial scale and projecting future OI burdens under climate change scenarios...The study reveals a substantial cumulative OI risk ranging between 5.1 and 29.0 % during heatwaves, with outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas showing heightened vulnerability. Younger workers, males, injury-related claims, and workers in areas with higher temperatures and less vegetation were identified as particularly susceptible to HWs...Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI... These findings emphasise the importance of developing localized, climate-related adaptation and intervention strategies to safeguard workers and reduce both the disease burden and associated economic costs and productivity loss."; ===;  ; "To assess the impacts of climate change on the water resources of the United States, we used hydrological models at the scale of U.S. Geological Survey Hydrologic Unit Code (HUC) 8 watersheds.";  ;  ;  ; "Climate change adaptation refers to adjustments in ecological, social, or economic systems in response to actual or expected climate effects or their impacts. It refers to changes in processes, practices, and structures to moderate potential damages or to benefit from opportunities associated with climate change." "Adaptation actions range from incremental adjustments to transformative changes." "Limits to adaptation also exist, and some adaptation actions can lead to maladaptation." "Effective adaptation requires integrating climate change considerations into planning and decision-making across different sectors and scales." "Barriers to adaptation include financial constraints, institutional capacity, and social equity concerns."
Drivers of Risk: Younger workers; males; injury-related claims; higher temperatures; less vegetation; outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas.
Methodology: Employs a time series study design and distributed lag nonlinear models to assess the association between heatwaves (using Excess Heat Factor) and occupational injuries/illnesses (OI) in four Australian cities at the Statistical Area Level 3 (SA3), utilizing high-resolution geospatial data and future projections to estimate future OI burdens under climate change scenarios.
Highlights: Heatwaves significantly increase occupational injuries and illnesses (OI) with risks ranging from 5.1-29.0%, disproportionately impacting younger, male workers in hotter, less vegetated peri-urban areas; climate change projections show increased OI attributable fractions, necessitating localized adaptation strategies.
More Notes: Heatwaves significantly impact occupational injuries and illnesses in Australian cities, with spatial analyses revealing higher risks in outer and peri-urban areas; younger workers, males, and those in high-temperature or low-vegetation areas are most vulnerable, emphasizing the need for localized adaptation strategies as future projections indicate increased occupational risks under high-emission scenarios.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is mentioned as a key response to climate change, focusing on strategies that reduce vulnerability and build resilience. The papers discuss various adaptation actions and their effectiveness, but also highlight limits to adaptation and potential maladaptation. Consideration is given to both incremental and transformative adaptation approaches across different sectors and scales, and the importance of integrating adaptation into planning and decision-making processes is emphasized. Barriers to effective adaptation, such as financial constraints, institutional capacity, and social equity concerns are also identified.

Drivers of Risk: Rising global temperatures; Increased habitat water temperature; Acute thermal stress; Chronic thermal stress.
Methodology: A systematic review was conducted to understand the biochemical responses of thermal stress on fish.
Highlights: Rising water temperatures stress fish, triggering biochemical responses from neurotransmitter/hormonal changes to mortality; fish cope via gene expression, metabolite alterations, and enzyme activity modulation; prolonged stress induces oxidative damage and increased energy demands, impacting growth, reproduction, and immunity; heat shock proteins offer some protection.
More Notes: Thermal stress from rising water temperatures negatively impacts fish physiology from molecular to organismal levels, causing reduced growth, reproductive issues, increased disease susceptibility, and mortality, necessitating further research for mitigation strategies.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation strategies involve fish altering gene expression, metabolite profiles, cellular signaling, and enzyme activity to cope with thermal stress. These biochemical and molecular responses, such as the production of heat shock proteins and warm acclimation proteins, are seen as protective mechanisms that help maintain protein integrity and cellular function under stress. The study emphasizes the need for further research into these mechanisms to inform effective mitigation strategies.

This review article focuses on the compilation of IPM methods, combining agro-ecological practices and biological control agents such as parasitoids and viruses, for the effective management of FAW. Approaches such as intercropping, agronomic practices, and the use of parasitoids and viruses have shown promising results in controlling FAW. This review article provides insights into successful management methods, recommendations and suggestions for the sustainable control of FAW using agro-ecological practices, biological control agents or their combination.
Drivers of Risk: Invasive alien insects; rapid reproduction; adaptation; high distribution; crop devastation; ecological damage; economic losses; climate change; favorable conditions.
Methodology: The review compiles integrated pest management (IPM) methods, combining agro-ecological practices (e.g., intercropping, agronomic practices) and biological control agents (parasitoids, viruses), for effective fall armyworm (FAW) management.
Highlights: Fall armyworm (FAW) poses a significant threat to global agriculture due to its rapid reproduction, adaptability, and distribution, exacerbated by climate change; integrated pest management (IPM) strategies using agro-ecological practices and biological control agents like parasitoids and viruses offer promising sustainable control methods.
More Notes: Invasive alien insects, particularly fall armyworm (FAW), pose a major threat to agriculture due to climate change-driven conditions favoring their spread; integrated pest management (IPM) strategies combining agro-ecological practices and biological control agents offer promising sustainable control methods.
Adaptation considered: The article discusses integrated pest management (IPM) methods as a form of adaptation against invasive alien insects like the fall armyworm. It highlights the transition from ineffective pesticide use to strategies involving agro-ecological practices and biological control agents, which are essential components of adapting agricultural systems to climate-driven pest invasions. These methods include intercropping, specific agronomic practices, and employing natural enemies like parasitoids and viruses.

Drivers of Risk: Dry spells, drought (SPEI), aridity, heat waves, water stress (short-term, medium-term, long-term), joint water and heat stress.
Methodology: The study conducts a comprehensive global panel analysis from 1979 to 2016, examining the influence of dry spells on crop production by analyzing a dataset of daily precipitation and temperature gridded to a 0.5-degree resolution. It defines dry spells as sequences of abnormally dry days that exceed certain durations, using multiple minimum thresholds for both dry spells and heat waves. The methodology includes weighted calculations of dry spell days based on cropland area, spatial aggregation of dry spell exposure using irrigation and crop data, and the use of fixed effects and distributed lag models. The analysis assesses the interaction of dry spells with heat waves and other environmental factors such as drought and aridity, employing a two-stage sequential g-estimation to control for mediation effects. The study uses country-specific fixed effects, and conducts robustness checks using alternative model specifications, weighting schemes, and lagged variables to account for potential influences. Sub-Saharan Africa is excluded due to data concerns, and the findings are statistically confirmed using bootstrapping techniques.
Highlights: Dry spells lower crop production growth, with larger effects when co-occurring with heat waves, drought, or aridity.
More Notes: The FAO CPI is a price-weighted sum of crop quantities; distinguishing direct from indirect dry spell effects is crucial, especially with increasing drought frequency; high temperatures exacerbate dry spell impacts, particularly in arid environments; irrigation may aid adaptation, but causality isn't determined; crop production growth is more adversely affected during drought conditions; results are based on 28+ day dry spells with country-clustered standard errors; understanding model specifications and rationales is essential; the paper presents controlled direct effects, isolating dry spell impacts; dry spell effects are statistically significant at p < 0.05 for SPEI = -2 and SPEI = -1.
Adaptation considered: Irrigation availability is investigated as a moderator of dry spell impacts. Heterogeneous effects are explored based on aridity, drought, and irrigation, with models adjusting for precipitation. Split-sample and interaction-term approaches are used to understand effects of dry spells based on aridity and drought. Effects in irrigated and non-irrigated cells are compared. Spatial weighting by crop area and cereal production accounts for irrigation's baseline productivity effects. Sequential g-estimation is used to estimate controlled direct effects in the presence of drought. Lagged agricultural outputs and dry spell effects are considered.

Outcome(s) of Interest: Disruptions to marine food webs, biochemical processes, human health, fisheries, aquaculture, and tourism.

Drivers of Risk: Wind patterns, increased primary productivity, warming conditions (Australian East Coast).
Methodology: Boosted Regression Trees, trained with high-resolution environmental data and field data on *Physalia physalis* occurrence (North Atlantic: 2008–2021; Southeast Pacific: 2016–2020), were used to model drivers and patterns, hindcast occurrences over 30 years, and assess the relative contribution of wind patterns, primary productivity, and warming conditions.
Highlights: Region-specific wind patterns and increased primary productivity drive Portuguese man o' war occurrence in the North Atlantic and Southeast Pacific, with warming conditions also contributing on the Australian East Coast; hindcasting reveals contrasting trends of decadal fluctuations in the Azores and increased occurrence on the Australian East Coast, suggesting future blooms may increase due to anticipated productivity and temperature changes, impacting ecosystems, human health, and the economy.
More Notes: Portuguese man o' war blooms are driven by wind patterns and primary productivity, with warming conditions also contributing on the Australian East Coast; future changes in productivity and temperature may increase blooms, impacting ecosystems, human health, and the economy.
Adaptation considered: The study highlights the importance of understanding jellyfish occurrence to develop effective management strategies. The insights into drivers and patterns of occurrence, particularly the role of wind, productivity, and warming, can support coastal management. The anticipation of increased blooms due to future changes in productivity and temperature suggests a need for adaptation measures to mitigate impacts on ecosystems, human health, and the economy.

Effects are most severe in Asia and Africa, which also have the lowest adaptive capacity.

Page 4: Such growing acute exposure is a cause for great concern - in particular for older individuals - and it will likely drive up the demand for and use of indoor thermal regulation appliances, with significant private and social costs for energy use and its externalities and health repercussions for those who cannot afford them.

Page 8: Highlights: "Many 69+ individuals are disproportionately vulnerable to the effects of heat extremes due to chronic health conditions and illnesses, physical, sensory, or cognitive disabilities, and social isolation, marginalization, and lacking access to healthcare or resources for private adaptation."

Page 8: Drivers of Risk: "Increasing building albedo and/or enlarging green spaces and tree canopy cover to counteract climate change-driven enhancement of the urban heat island effect."

Page 12: Evaluation overview: The page mentions studies assessing "future heat-related mortality under climate change scenarios" and "population aging and heat exposure in the 21st century" for U.S. regions.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, population aging, heat exposure, demographic changes, urban heat island, rising temperatures, social and health services demands.
Category: Health > Heat and Cold (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, etc.)
Methodology: The study combines age-stratified demographic projections with downscaled CMIP6 temperature projections to mid-century (2050) to quantify chronic and acute heat exposure for different age groups globally, focusing on the population aged 69+.
Highlights: Climate change will be the prevailing mechanism of change in more temperate nations of Europe and North America, while population aging will be the defining driver of future exposure in warmer countries of Africa, Asia, and South America.
More Notes: This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, allowing use, sharing, adaptation, distribution, and reproduction with proper credit.
Adaptation considered: The extent of adaptation is considered through demographic projections combined with climate models to assess heat exposure for different age groups, focusing notably on older adults aged 69+ who are more vulnerable. The analysis identifies regional vulnerabilities and indicates adaptation strategies, like increasing building albedo and expanding green space, to mitigate heat island effects. The potential for rising demand in thermal regulation appliances for adaptation is acknowledged, along with associated energy and health costs. There are also efforts to quantify the impact on morbidity and mortality, highlighting a research gap in understanding adaptation costs and effectiveness.
